Unless I misunderstand you, this is the expected behavior of `furl.args`.

furl.args is a dictionary (an [orderedmultidict](https://github.com/gruns/orderedmultidict) to be specific), and just like a
standard Python dictionary, `KeyError` is raised on a missing key.

```python
>>> d = {1:1}
>>> d[2]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
KeyError: 2
>>>
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/?1=1')
>>> dict(f.args)
{'1': '1'}
>>> f.args[2]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
KeyError: 2
```

To avoid raising a KeyError on a missing key, use the `get()` method, just like
`get()` with a standard Python dictionary.

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/?1=1')
>>> dict(f.args)
{'1': '1'}
>>> print(f.args.get(2))
None
```

Does that answer your question?

The way I've designed HTTP mock matchers and the way I've integrated it from mock DSL perspective doesn't force me the proxy mock expectations in an equivalent `mock._request` instance, unless you explicitely use it.

In other words, that's something it can be relatively easy to fix, but adding complexibility in multiple areas. The current code is pretty simple because it doesn't have to delegate mock definitions in two places.

Also note that `mock._request` is a private member. It's not intended to be API level consumable by the user.

Would changing this help you?

> After wiring up the data pins, you have to connect the backlight and set up the contrast circuit. As there are some differences regarding the hardware between different panels, please refer to `this tutorial <https://learn.adafruit.com/character-lcds/wiring-a-character-lcd>`__ to learn how to wire up these circuits. Hi,

Interesting. My day job is a network engineer - primarily enterprise and data center LAN.

There are a couple of gmpy2 features that are a bit non-standard but they may help you with bit level manipulations. An mpz type can be indexed by the bit positions. For example:

```
>>> b=gmpy2.mpz(12345678)
>>> b.digits(2)
'101111000110000101001110'
>>> b[10:20].digits(2)
'1100011000'
>>> b[20:40].digits(2)
'1011'
```

If you try to index beyond the left-most bit, an infinite number of 0 bits is assumed.

There is also a mutable integer type called xmpz. With an xmpz, you can do in-place left/right shifts and modify bits - either individually or in slices.

I have written custom private functions for other libraries. Would an _xmpz_import() and _xmpz_export() functions be sufficient? (Or _mpz_import()/_mpz_export()?) I may change them to public functions in the future but keeping them as private functions for now will make it easier to change them if needed.

Regards,
Case

You can just define your stochastic parameter outside of the lambda function and call it inside of it, providing random_state as a parameter.

```python
import imgaug as ia
from imgaug import augmenters as iaa
from imgaug import parameters as iap
import numpy as np

add = iap.DiscreteUniform(-100, 100)

def func_images(images, random_state, parents, hooks):
    images = [image.astype(np.int32) for image in images]
    nb_images = len(images)
    samples = add.draw_samples(nb_images, random_state)
    for i, sample in enumerate(samples):
        images[i] += sample
    images = [np.clip(image, 0, 255).astype(np.uint8) for image in images]
    return images

def func_heatmaps(heatmaps, random_state, parents, hooks):
    return heatmaps

def func_keypoints(keypoints_on_images, random_state, parents, hooks):
    return keypoints_on_images
    
aug = iaa.Lambda(
    func_images=func_images,
    func_heatmaps=func_heatmaps,
    func_keypoints=func_keypoints
)
aug.show_grid(ia.quokka_square(size=(128, 128)), 5, 5)
```

> Can I somehow import annotations from parsers?

FLAT uses the FoLiA format. Some parsers like [Frog](https://github.io/frog) output this natively, for some others there are converters (e.g. Alpino), for others you may need to implement a converter yourself. I'm planning on working on a converter from the CONNL-U format if that helps.

> Do you have some kind of documentation, how to build customized annotation schemes?

Yes, see the documentation on FoLiA Set Definitions, chapter 3 of the [FoLiA Documentation](https://github.com/proycon/folia/blob/master/docs/folia.pdf). Also see the examples in https://github.com/proycon/folia/tree/master/setdefinitions 

> Is there a possibility to see my annotations as arcs?

Not really at nicely visualised arcs no, the best we have is the visualisations you can toggle on/off in the global annotations menu.

Also be aware that the default `Full` configuration of FLAT exposes a lot of functionality you don't need and which will confuse the annotator, make sure to customize your configuration to enable only those features you need (see the administration guide), I might have to ship some simpler configurations with FLAT out of the box.

I suspect this issue is caused by removal of  `None` initializer special treatment in pyasn1 types constructors. Before 0.4.x you could do:

    DnAttributes(None)

Starting pyasn1 0.4.x you can only do:

    DnAttributes()
    # ... which is equivalent to
    DnAttributes(pyasn1.type.univ.noValue)

Since `DnAttributes` type has a default value (`False`), it will be used as initializer in all the cases above. So the fix would be either:

    if filter_node.assertion['dnAttributes'] is not None:
        matching_filter['dnAttributes'] = DnAttributes(filter_node.assertion['dnAttributes'])
    else:
        matching_filter['dnAttributes'] = DnAttributes()

Or better initilaize `filter_node.assertion['dnAttributes']` to `pyasn1.type.univ.noValue` instead of `None` by default. Would it be possible with ldap3?

That is not possible afaik. The problem is that the conversion between Lab and RGB is not invertible. Consider the first test:
```
lab_pixel(0,0,0) [Lab(128,-128,0)] to RGB -> (0, 64, 194)
RGB(0, 64, 194) to Lab -> Lab(33,36,-71)[lab_pixel(84,164,56)]
```
but 
```
Lab(33,36,-71)[lab_pixel(84,164,56)] to RGB -> (0, 64, 194)
```

The problem is that multiple Lab values are mapped to same RGB value, and only some of the Lab values are actually invertible.

@OranjeeGeneral I believe it is. I'm adding up the gradient at each of the pixels that got upsampled. If

```
a | b
c | d
```
transforms to 
```
a | a | b | b
a | a | b | b
c | c | d | d
c | c | d | d
```
then the gradient coming back along each of these would be
```
partial a/partial f | partial a/partial f | partial b/partial f | partial b/partial f
partial a/partial f | partial a/partial f | partial b/partial f | partial b/partial f
partial c/partial f | partial c/partial f | partial d/partial f | partial d/partial f
partial c/partial f | partial c/partial f | partial d/partial f | partial d/partial f
```
and you would end up with (at the front of the upsampling layer):
```
4(partial a/partial f) | 4(partial b/partial f)
4(partial c/partial f) | 4(partial d/partial f)
```
Since the upsample layer contributed to the error along each of the upsampled pixels (because it controlled what went there) it gets the full gradient of all the pixels in each upsampled block.

By default, pytypes treats an unassigned typevar ``T`` as *unknown* in the sense that nothing is a subtype. (*unkown* is however subtype of ``Any``). By providing a writable dict for ``bound_typevars`` you allow pytypes to bind ``T`` as needed. (I recently enhanced [doc of ``is_subtype``](https://github.com/Stewori/pytypes/blob/master/pytypes/type_util.py#L1442) where this is now explained)
You can afterwards see how pytypes has bound ``T``:
```
bt = {}
print is_subtype(EntirelyParam2, FullUnparam[T, int], bound_typevars=bt) # True
print bt # {~T: <type 'str'>}
```
I didn't apply this in my draft of ``get_subclasses``, because it would have resulted in additional subclasses to be added, contrary to the examples you denoted.

out of a dozen, exactly ONE was working.
So the script was not entirely stalled, and also the `wait('shift-X')` at the end was working.
After puzzling around for a while, I realized that the keyword for that working abbrev was probably the only one made of characters from the QWERTY layout (i.e. not a, z, m, w, x, numbers etc.).
So I remapped all the other keywords to simple double chars (avoiding those), and sure enough, they mostly could be made to expand... but interpreted in QWERTY.
So it's another layout problem again, a probably a hairy one.

Amongst other things it comes with a generous dose of keyboard overlays to make its use more PC-like.
I don't know how it works exactly (probably several layers & tools involved), but there's a fair chance that it's playing havoc with your code here.

colorama is meant to work on both Linux and Windows and convert supported ANSI styles to Windows code where possible. While it is possible to produce italic style on some Unix terminals like this:

    print '\x1b[3mThis is in italic!\x1b[0m'

Windows doesn't have a matching console attribute to produce a similar effect ([MSDN](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms682088(v=vs.85).aspx#_win32_character_attributes)). Therefore there are no plans to add this style to colorama. If you want to support Unix terminals only, you can always manually add the ANSI codes like above.
See also the issue about other possible ANSI styles that we could not add: 
https://github.com/tartley/colorama/issues/38#issuecomment-128834060

Sorry for the late update here. No, you're not doing anything wrong with the query, it's just the way that indexes (and sorting) are implemented (mostly operations over Redis data structures). For indexes as they are built, you're best off limiting the number of results you want in some way via filtering, before sorting them - otherwise everything will be sorted (in Redis) prior to return.

Alternatively, if you want to perform queries more or less directly against indexes (without copies, intersects, etc.), the `model.get_by()` method lets you query for specific value ranges over existing indexes, and offers a hidden `_limit=(offset, count)` option for pagination. So `MyModel.get_by(timestamp=(start, end), _limit=(0, 1000))` to get the first 1000 items by ordered timestamp between the provided `start` and `end` time periods.

This issue drove me nuts for a couple hours until I found out it wasn't me or my system's fault. +1 for a fix please! This library is just what I needed otherwise.

For future reference (and if you may need to deal with Xoran XCAT ENT Scanners) this is how I defined a call-back that checks if the `tag` is the problematic Exposure and if that contains a dot (`b"."`), I issue a user warning and round that value. Other measures like changing the type of the field are possible. 

```python

def fix_round_floats_in_IS_fields(raw_elem):
    if raw_elem.tag == (0x0018, 0x1152):
        if b"." in raw_elem.value:
            rounded = round(float(raw_elem.value))
            import warnings
            warnings.warn("Floating point value {0} in 'Exposure' field! Rounding to {1}".
                          format(raw_elem.value, rounded))
            raw_elem = raw_elem._replace(value=bytes(str(int(rounded)), 'utf8'))
    return raw_elem

pydicom.config.data_element_callback = fix_round_floats_in_IS_fields

```

Especially using the `_replace` method fields a bit hacky.

Hi @arminorlik,

this issue is not related to ecs-deploy, i think it's about configuring ECS in general.

As I understand, your containers have at least one constraint (e.g. a fixed port), which causes that only one container can be executed on a single instance at a time, right?

This problem can be solved by setting appropriate values in the deployment configuration in the service. What are the current values for "Minimum healthy percent" and "Maximum percent"?

If you have 3 instances and a desired count of 2, the "Maximum percent" has to be 150, which means there will never be more than 3 containers (150% of 2) running at the same time. 
The "Minimum healthy percent" can be 100% or less. If you chose 50%, the deployment will probably be faster.

This is what should happen with this config and a desired count of 2:
- ECS starts one new container => 150% of 2
- ECS stops one old container => 100% of 2
- ECS starts the second new container => 150% of 2
- ECS stops the second old container => 100% of 2
- done.

If "Maximum percent" is e.g. 200%, ECS first tries to spin up 2 new containers, which fails, because there are only 3 instances in the cluster. Then you receive the error mentioned above.

I hope that was helpful.

Best
Fabian

Okay so this isn't a problem is the change made to that if statement, the issue is that you need to make the image a PIL Image object before running it through pytesseract. If you run pytesseract from the command line it does this for you but if you directly call the pytesseract.image_to_string method in a python script then you need to make it a PIL Image object yourself. Here is an example code on how to do this:

```
import pytesseract
import PIL.Image as Image

print(pytesseract.image_to_string(Image.open('/**path_to_picture**/digits.png')))
```
This successfully prints the characters in the image

Hey, @bjorgvino's solution is a good one that I have used with autoenv several times with good experiences. If anyone has this same same frustration with Conda environments, here is the equivalent. Add this to your .env file:
```
venv=<Write your conda environment name here (without angle braces)>
currentvenv=""
if [[ $CONDA_PREFIX != "" ]]
then
  # Strip out the path and just leave the env name
  currentvenv="${CONDA_PREFIX##*/}"
fi
if [[ "$currentvenv" != "$venv" ]]
then
  echo "Switching to environment: $venv"
  #workon $venv
  source activate $venv
else
  echo Still in Conda environment $venv
fi
```
I also like to add these lines to my .env to avoid having to type `source activate <environment name>`, and allow me to instead just type `activate` (same for `deactivate`):
```
activate() {
  source activate $venv
}
deactivate() {
  source deactivate $venv
}
```

Hi @sjpatil & @EnderShadow , lets take a look at the code for `basic_example.py`.

The goal of this line is to get a response to the input "How are you?". However, it is important to keep in mind that this chat bot is untrained at this point, so it doesn't know **anything** about the world or how to communicate. The first thing we do to it is say "How are you?" so now, it knows one thing (that phrase). So, the only thing it can do is respond back with that input.

```python
response = chatbot.get_response("How are you?")

print(response)
```

Ok, so let's try something else. If we train the bot, it can learn more about the structure of responses.
Here is another example:

```python
from chatterbot.trainers import ListTrainer

chatbot.set_trainer(ListTrainer)

# Tran the chat bot  with an example conversation
chatbot.train([
    "How are you?",
    "I am good",
])

response = chatbot.get_response("How are you?")

print(response)
```

Now that we have trained the bot with a bit of information, the response it will return when given "How are you" is "I am good".

I hope this helps. Please let me know if you encounter any other issues.

Hi everyone - I just found this thread after I posted a similar issues

I wrote a python repo (https://github.com/met-office-lab/conversation-skill) for Alexa dialogues a while back which has functionality for robust dialogues. I wonder if its approach is similar - or will help add to the debate.

The idea is that, for a given intent, the slots have their own prompts, help etc attached to them. So if you trigger an intent with 3 slots say, but you only provide information for one slot, then it walks over the missing slots, robustly asking (with context sensitive help etc) until all the slots are satisfied, then it triggers the original intent.

We took an object orientated approach, with the prompt, reprompt, card and help all associated with the Intent object, but crucially also with each the Intent's Slots.

e.g.
Me: "What time's the next bus from Exeter to Plymouth?"
Alexa: "14:20"

but the same code will also do

Me: " What time's the next bus?"
Alexa: "Where are you?"
Me: "What do you mean?"
Alexa: "I mean where are you starting your journey?"
Me: "Exeter"
Alexa: "Where are you going"
Me: "Plymouth"
Alexa: "14:20"

I ran into this as well, I believe that the behavior difference seen by users/devs in #14 is due to "USE_TZ" being set differently in their project's settings file.  This setting will make the model timezone aware if set to True, however "now" is always being created as timezone unaware. [See the documentation here.](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/i18n/timezones/).

As a quick test, can you flip the current value to see if that fixes your issue?  If USE_TZ is not defined in your settings.py then it is defaulting to False, so USE_TZ=True would be the opposite value.

The above helped me solve my problem, which is, I think, the same one the OP raised, namely how to do stuff to handle errors without blocking the caller. In my case the caller is a Kivy UI thread. So thanks for the clue.

The following may be a bit too verbose, but it demonstrates one way to get the job done. The whole business with re-raising the caught exception is just my way of visualizing the flow clearly in the context of the original post. In my real code I expect to map the exception classes to handlers and re-raise any unmapped exceptions in the caller's callback. 

I also expect to add more context in the "pending" records so as to allow retry in an intelligent way (see #26 for more thoughts on that). My ultimate goal is to have reliable interaction with a server that might occasionally get balky, or when there is a short network issue (slightly unreliable wifi is involved in the application).

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
from pprint import pprint
import time
from requests_futures.sessions import FuturesSession
from requests.exceptions import ConnectTimeout, HTTPError, ReadTimeout

class Bar(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.session = FuturesSession()
        self.pending = []

    def cb(self, future):
        index = 0
        callback = None
        for pend in self.pending:
            if pend['future'] == future:
                discard = self.pending.pop(index)
                callback = pend['callback']
                break
            index += 1
        if callback is None:
            raise Exception('logic error, no record of future')
        exc = future.exception()
        if exc is not None:
            try:
                print('got exception {}'.format(exc))
                raise exc
            except ConnectTimeout:
                print('got connect timeout, increasing timeout for retry')
                new_future = self.session.get('http://httpbin.org/get',
                                              timeout=2.0)

                new_future.add_done_callback(self.cb)
                pend = dict(future=new_future, callback=callback)
                self.pending.append(pend)
                return
            except ReadTimeout as e:
                print('got read timeout, increasing timeout for retry')
                new_future = self.session.get('http://httpbin.org/delay/1', timeout=3)
                new_future.add_done_callback(self.cb)
                pend = dict(future=new_future, callback=callback)
                self.pending.append(pend)
                return
            except Exception as e:
                callback(None, e)
        else:
            print('request got no exception')
            res = future.result()
            if res.status_code != 200:
                print('bad status code {}'.format(res.status_code))
                try:
                    res.raise_for_status()
                except Exception as e:
                    callback(None, e)
                    return
            callback(res)

    def get_connect_timeout(self, callback):
        future = self.session.get('http://httpbin.org/get',
                                  timeout=0.01,)
        future.add_done_callback(self.cb)
        pend = dict(future=future, callback=callback)
        self.pending.append(pend)

    def get_read_timeout(self, callback):
        future = self.session.get('http://httpbin.org/delay/5', timeout=1)
        future.add_done_callback(self.cb)
        pend = dict(future=future, callback=callback)
        self.pending.append(pend)

    def get_bad_status(self, callback):
        future = self.session.get('http://httpbin.org/status/502')
        future.add_done_callback(self.cb)
        pend = dict(future=future, callback=callback)
        self.pending.append(pend)

bar = Bar()
done = False

def cb(res, exc=None):
    global done
    if res:
        print('got callback with res {}'.format(res))
    else:
        print('got callback with exception {}'.format(exc))
    done = True

done = False
bar.get_connect_timeout(cb)
time.sleep(0.1)
while not done:
    time.sleep(1)
    print(time.time())

done = False
bar.get_read_timeout(cb)
time.sleep(0.1)
while not done:
    time.sleep(1)
    print(time.time())


done = False
bar.get_bad_status(cb)
time.sleep(0.1)
while not done:
    time.sleep(1)
    print(time.time())
```

Now email content (subject, text, html) has been moved into its own abstract base class, which is shared by ``EmailTemplate`` and ``TranslatedEmailTemplate``.
First I tried to keep all these fields inside ``TranslatedEmailTemplate``. The big problem then however is, that its impossible to force the administrator to create some default content. Even, if you add a *default* ``TranslatedEmailTemplate``, you have no guarantee that its not deleted. The validation functions, such as ``clean()`` can't perform these checks.

Therefore IHMO the only safe solution, if you insist on a fallback template, is to store the fields ``subject``, ``text`` and ``html`` twice. Everything else does more harm than benefit.

There are a couple of other apps that use that approach.
I used a few several years ago, and remember it being a total mess. Maybe there are better things that appeared since then (I really don't remember what I used before post office, it was too long ago).

Using Django templates usually creates it's own mess. You'll need 3 template files for every email (subject, text content, html content) and a specific directory structure. Handling errors is also complicated.

What I did in my last project, was creating a management command that loads django templates from files and creates/updates post_office EmailTemplate objects based on them.
This way I'm keeping the templates in version control, and load them to the DB when deploying, so all the errors are also caught there.

I can post here the code for the management command if it's useful to anyone.
I doubt it's something that @selwin will want in the project... but maybe it can be improved a bit and packaged into an external dependency.

Aside from the standard library usage case, the other main reason for not wanting to import the `typing` module is because it takes a long time to import (around 10 ms on my machine). That's a fairly high price to pay just to get access to a spelling that could be handled via a simple class instance, without all of the `typing` modules metaclass gymnastics.

If you combine it with the `is_class_var` singledispatch function, and move the typing.ClassVar registration into the typing module, then I think you'd be establishing a good precedent whereby:

- `dataclasses` had aliases for stable, well-defined typing concepts that static analysers understood, but did *not* interact with the runtime type machinery (since they'd produce ordinary instances, not runtime classes)
- `typing` paid the runtime price for trying to make objects that "did the right thing" when interacting with the runtime type machinery.

In essense, where we now have `typing` and `typing_extensions`, we'd move to a situation where we instead have `dataclasses` in the standard library and `typing` as a bundled PyPI module. There'd be a *very* strict filter on things "graduating" from `typing` into `dataclasses`, but there'd also be a well-defined subset of the type annotations where the semantics were considered completely stable (with `ClassVar`, and potentially `Any`, being the only initial members of that set).

"""

    we will have to wire them together in the right order.

    So this is what happens here.
"""

from pipeline.storage import PipelineMixin
from require.storage import OptimizedFilesMixin
from django.contrib.staticfiles.storage import ManifestStaticFilesStorage


class HackStorage(
        PipelineMixin, OptimizedFilesMixin, ManifestStaticFilesStorage):

```

The problem might actually be specific to certain citation styles in bibtex. The thing is, I assume bibtex does not know about latex macros at all (how should it, when even the control character can be redefined in tex). Thus it simply splits the last names of authors at spaces.

In my particular problem, I work around it by monkey patching `latexcodec`. I did not test, but I'm afraid `Y{\i }lmaz` might get converted to `Y{\i~}lmaz`. In any case, my goal is to have a unicode capable toolchain that does not require manual intervention, so the other option would be to enclose every non-ascii character in braces before conversion.

I'm aware that bracing changes the capitalisation semantics of bibtex, but how would the inserted empty brackets cause problems in bibtex? My impression was that most online resources seem to suggest the use of braces to delimit macros (at least in bibtex). So I think `latexcodec` could profit from at least a configuration option to choose macro delimiting (maybe directly to the codecs instances, not necessarily to the globally registered one). The patch to `get_space_bytes` should be easy even in the presence of configuration options (i.e. I could do it and sumit a PR), but for the table it's not immediately obvious to me how to proceed. On Sep 17, 2017 9:34 AM, "Werner Robitza" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> If you have 10 minutes of someone speaking, there will be pauses in the
> audio that are very low in volume. The dynamic range is the difference
> between the maximum volume and the minimum volume, hence, there will be
> quite some dynamic range in your file.
>
> Peak normalization (e.g., normalizing to a peak value of 0 dBFS in the
> following example) only lifts the gain, but the dynamic range of the file
> will be the same:
>
> [image: image]
> <https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/582444/30521800-a3f7f3a8-9bc5-11e7-8b50-569d51b71352.png>
>
> If you want to minimize the dynamic range you need a heavy compressor or
> some other form of dynamic normalization where only *parts* (windows) of
> that waveform are normalized. Note that if you do that too extremely you'll
> just amplify the noise floor of the recording, meaning that you'll get a
> lot of random noise in speaker pauses.
>
> (Sorry for the simple drawing.)

Thanks for the complete explanation of aspects for proposed changes.

Reading your comment, I agree on the solution being fragile and may cause unwanted side-effects, maybe it would have been better if the locale only affected that specific call. But again we're changing the original signature of method, comparing to original strftime().

> I think a safer approach here is to configure the instance's locale by accepting the locale in __init__ 
Actually this was our first idea when solving the issue, but as you've already mentioned, the date instance has been initialized way before the method call, hence changing the attribute would not have any effects.
(unless we dig dipper into the code and other methods).

Given the discussion, I think there are two options:
1. Modify changes in strftime() to make sure it only affects that specific call only (fixing the fragility). 
2. Add an `locale` parameter to `__init__` and in application while processing the date objects, make copy of them and change their locale on-the-fly. (In my case, this may mean I need to modify other packages such as django-jalali)

I'm thinking option 1.

What is your opinion?

Also, if you are willing to accept hitting the database twice, there is no need to return a list at all:
``` 
dup_list = []
last_version = None
for version in reversion.get_for_object(target_object) \
						.order_by('serialized_data', '-pk'):
	if last_version and last_version.serialized_data == version.serialized_data:
		dup_list.append(version.pk)
	last_version = version
versions = reversion.get_for_object(target_object) \
					.exclude(pk__in=dup_list)    
```
This same logic could be used for a management command in order to remove all duplicates in database. If you order by `serialized_data` you can even run the logic against all Version instances, no matter what object they are related to.

Hi @RingWong,

The issue is that when you take a vertical picture on your iPhone, the iPhone actually stores the image data sideways. It then records an EXIF tag in the image that tells the program reading the image that it needs to rotate the image itself to make it appear the correct way on screen. So when you open the image on your phone or computer, the view program knows to apply this rotation so the picture doesn't look sideways. However, face_recognition doesn't do that rotation automatically. So face_recognition doesn't detect any faces because the image is actually upside down or sideways.

You can find more info on EXIF image rotation here: http://leancrew.com/all-this/2009/04/derotating-jpegs-with-exiftool/

When I get some time, I want to write a little python library that fixes this problem. But you can also google for how to fix exif rotation in python code and find some examples.

Thanks for the report. I'm not sure what to make of it yet, and I haven't determined if it's a bug in either of our packages, or just a limitation of the namespace packages. I'll just write up a few notes that may or may not have much useful to add to this ticket, but may help me or some other person who would like to track this down in the future.

First, it's good to note that we aren't using implicit namespace packages as introduced in Python 3.3, because they wouldn't work on older Pythons, which would obviously defeat the purpose of backports.

The second thing to note is that we're declaring the backports namespace in two different ways. The way prescribed by the `backports` package, and by the `backports.shutil_get_terminal_size` package, is `extend_path`

```python
from pkgutil import extend_path
__path__ = extend_path(__path__, __name__)
```

While I use this method, which requires setuptools, but appears to be more modern and capable:

```python
__import__('pkg_resources').declare_namespace(__name__)
```

Because I'm not doing the same thing as prescribed in the parent module, it's feasible that this is causing strange errors, but I haven't been able to identify the cause of the issues yet.

Finally, I'll note that `--user` is a relatively new feature to the landscape of Python packaging, and it's not always clear what features are compatible with it, so it seems reasonably likely for it to be due to just using `--user` combined with system packages in the same namespace package. Again, I've not confirmed this yet, so I don't feel comfortable placing the blame there yet, and I'll instead leave this issue open for further investigation.

It's not possible. What you're describing is called a one cancels other (OCO) order which binance doesn't support. Bitfinex can do it. Maybe some other sites, I'm not sure. 

If you're collecting current price data in python you can calculate which of the two conditions is closer to being met and manually place the corresponding trade, then if the market moves back you cancel and place the other order. It's not perfect but better than not having it. 

Note: a naive implementation could cause you to place and cancel a tonne of trades if a market is hovering at the midpoint between your two conditions, flicking from one to the other with each data point that comes through.

val arr1 = Seq("a","b")

val r1 = Row(arr1)

```
def merge_two(x, y):
    """
    Merges two dictionaries, x and y. Same as `x.update(y)` except in the case where the values of
    x[key] and y[key] are both lists, in which case it joins the list values.

    Returns a copy of x, z, updated by y, where:
      z[key] = x[key] + y[key]
    """
    z = x.copy()
    for key, value in y.items():
        if isinstance(value, list) and isinstance(z.get(key, None), list):
            z[key] += value
        else:
            z[key] = value
    return z
```

Thanks for finding this. Yes, `add` is supposed to behave like a built-in `set` and not raise an exception for a duplicate value. Under the hood, it is implemented by calling the code path that does raise the error and just suppresses it when the `ValueError` is raised - line 84 in the stacktrace you posted. Because this raised a `TypeError` instead of the `ValueError` we were expecting, the exception wasn't caught. The bug is trivial, % string formatting treats tuples differently than other objects so creating the exception message (that would eventually be ignored in this case) threw an exception.

> overwriting event names that should contain characters illegal in method names is not possible (see @Namespace.event_name(...) in my implementation)

Yes. I documented that to use class based namespaces you have to use events that have legal method names. Having to replace characters in events makes the relationship between events and methods less clear, you can have two events going to the same method, for example. I prefer not to do that.

> Shouldn't Namespace.set_server be private to make it clear that one should use Server.register_namespace instead?

It's really not private, since another class is using it. I decided to leave it public, but not document it. It's really not a private method, since another class is using it.

> Doc string of Server.register_namespace mentions that you can pass either class or instance

Ah, yes, that is a mistake. I had it that way first, then decided against it. I'll fix it.

> This is a really conceptual question one could discuss about. Why do you use separate dicts for handlers and namespace_handlers? That way you can mix handlers of a Namespace object with those explicitly registered by Server.on for the same namespace. It could be useful for testing but maybe is a bit confusing. I stored both namespace objects and namespace dicts in one single Server.handlers dictionary and let Server._trigger_event decide how to look event handlers up for a given namespace..

Because with this you can override an event and have it go to a decorated function, while everything else goes to the class.

Hi - I've worked out why. Turns out that Django's default test runner runs all the migrations to setup the database for the first time.

If you have a test file that just looks like
```python
from django.test import TestCase
class EmptyTestCase(TestCase):
    pass
```
and have a migration that looks like
```python
from django.db import migrations
def forwards(apps, schema_editor):
    print '!go forwards!'

class Migration(migrations.Migration):
    operations = [migrations.RunPython(forwards)]
```
Then you'll get something like
```
❯ ./manage.py test 
Creating test database for alias 'default'...
!go forwards!

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 0 tests in 0.000s

OK
Destroying test database for alias 'default'...
```
So based on that, I think that this is expected behaviour!
There are [ways of getting Django not to run migrations to build the database, and instead to just create the database from the current state of models](http://stackoverflow.com/a/25267435).
Let me know if you find something to the contrary, or if this is causing a problem in your case.

Use `utf-8` instead of `ascii` encoding will fix this. 
I have tested several conditions, but I am not sure if it's enough.
The old version returns `text in self.get_source()` does work also.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python2
# coding: utf-8

#from __future__ import print_function
import unicodedata


''' The orgin method:

    def _is_text_present(self, text):
        text_norm = unicodedata.normalize(
            'NFD', text).encode('ascii', 'ignore')
        source_norm = unicodedata.normalize(
            'NFD', self.get_source()).encode('ascii', 'ignore')
        return text_norm in source_norm
'''

def test_contains(text, orgin_text, coding='utf-8'):
    normal_text = unicodedata.normalize('NFD', text)
    encoded_normal_text = normal_text.encode(coding, 'ignore')
    normal_orgin = unicodedata.normalize('NFD', orgin_text)
    encoded_normal_orgin = normal_orgin.encode(coding, 'ignore')
    print('''
    normal_text=[%s]
    encoded_text=[%s]
    normal_orgin=[%s]
    source_norm=[%s]
    '''
          % (normal_text.encode('utf-8'),
             encoded_normal_text,
             normal_orgin.encode('utf-8'),
             encoded_normal_orgin))
    return encoded_normal_text in encoded_normal_orgin

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # coding = utf-8
    # all clear when use utf-8/utf8 encoding
    assert test_contains(u'中文', u'我人有的和中文主产不为这'), 'Should contain'
    assert not test_contains(u'中文', u'我人有的和文主产不为这'), 'Should not contain'
    assert test_contains(u'english', u'qwertenglishyuiop'), 'Should contain'
    assert not test_contains(u'english', u'qwertenlishyuiop'), 'Should not contain'
    assert test_contains(u'Cœur', u'qwertCœuryuiop'), 'Should contain'
    assert test_contains(u'Colá', u'qwertColáyuiop'), 'Should contain'
    assert test_contains(u'Cola', u'qwertColáyuiop'), 'Should contain'
    assert test_contains(u'C\u0153ur', u'qwertC\u0153uryuiop'), 'Should contain'
    # coding = ascii
    # line +2 blow will fail when use ascii encoding
    assert test_contains(u'中文', u'我人有的和中文主产不为这', 'ascii'), 'Should contain'
    assert not test_contains(u'中文', u'我人有的和文主产不为这', 'ascii'), 'Should not contain'
    assert test_contains(u'english', u'qwertenglishyuiop', 'ascii'), 'Should contain'
    assert not test_contains(u'english', u'qwertenlishyuiop', 'ascii'), 'Should not contain'
    assert test_contains(u'Cœur', u'qwertCœuryuiop', 'ascii'), 'Should contain'
    assert test_contains(u'Colá', u'qwertColáyuiop', 'ascii'), 'Should contain'
    assert test_contains(u'Cola', u'qwertColáyuiop', 'ascii'), 'Should contain'
    assert test_contains(u'C\u0153ur', u'qwertC\u0153uryuiop', 'ascii'), 'Should contain'
```

I still think this is a bit weird to have two ways of doing this, but since it is part of the HTTP API and we mirror that pretty closely in this library it doesn't really hurt!

FWIW - your first approach iterates over a QueryResult which is a wrapper to the raw response which steps through the `resp['docs']` list and ignores anything else in the response.  While your second approach yields the entire raw response (`raw_result=True`) which consists of the `docs` list and that warning.  So I am pretty certain that your first approach actually has that warning as well in the original response, you just can't see it.  So the behaviors of your two approaches are likely the same.  To see the raw response for your first approach, rather than using `query1.result` you could just call `query1` directly since it is callable and is what `query1.result` calls and wraps as a QueryResult.  

In approach one, replacing:

```python
for doc in query1.result:
    print doc
```

with:

```python 
print query1
```

should be the same as `print (resp)` in your second approach.

Unfortunately that does not explain the warning but I think it should at least address your seemingly different results.

class get_vgg16(gluon.Block):
    def __init__(self, max, **kwargs):
        super(get_vgg16, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        # c2 layer

        self.c2.add(nn.Conv3D(64, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.c2.add(nn.Conv3D(64, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.c2.add(nn.MaxPool3D(pool_size=(2, 2, 2), strides = (2, 2, 2)))
        self.c2.add(nn.Conv3D(128, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.c2.add(nn.Conv3D(128, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        # c3 layer
        self.c3 = nn.Sequential(prefix='c3')
        self.c3.add(nn.MaxPool3D(pool_size=(2, 2, 2), strides = (2, 2, 2)))
        self.c3.add(nn.Conv3D(256, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.c3.add(nn.Conv3D(256, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.c3.add(nn.Conv3D(256, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        # c4 layer
        self.c4 = nn.Sequential(prefix='c4')
        self.c4.add(nn.MaxPool3D(pool_size=(2, 2, 2), strides = (2, 2, 2)))
        self.c4.add(nn.Conv3D(512, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.c4.add(nn.Conv3D(512, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.c4.add(nn.Conv3D(512, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        # c5 layer
        self.c5 = nn.Sequential(prefix='c5')
        self.c5.add(nn.MaxPool3D(pool_size=(2, 2, 2), strides = (2, 2, 2)))
        self.c5.add(nn.Conv3D(512, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.c5.add(nn.Conv3D(512, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        # u5 layer
        self.c5.add(nn.Conv3D(128, kernel_size = 1, padding = 0, activation='relu'))
        self.c5.add(nn.Conv3DTranspose(128, kernel_size = 2, strides = (2, 2, 2), activation='relu'))
        # x4 layer
        self.x4 = nn.Sequential(prefix='x4')
        self.x4.add(nn.Conv3D(128, kernel_size = 1, padding = 0, activation='relu'))
        # u4 layer
        self.u4 = nn.Sequential(prefix='u4')
        self.u4.add(nn.Conv3DTranspose(128, kernel_size = 2, strides = (2, 2, 2), activation='relu'))
        # x3 layer
        self.x3 = nn.Sequential(prefix='x3')
        self.x3.add(nn.Conv3D(128, kernel_size = 1, padding = 0, activation='relu'))
        # u3 layer
        self.u3 = nn.Sequential(prefix='u3')
        self.u3.add(nn.Conv3DTranspose(128, kernel_size = 2, strides = (2, 2, 2), activation='relu'))
        # x2 layer
        self.x2 = nn.Sequential(prefix='x2')
        self.x2.add(nn.Conv3D(128, kernel_size = 1, padding = 0, activation='relu'))
        # Block 7
        self.net = nn.Sequential(prefix='net')
        self.net.add(nn.Conv3D(256, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1, activation='relu'))
        self.net.add(nn.Conv3D(4096, kernel_size = 1, padding = 0, activation='relu'))
        self.net.add(nn.Conv3D(4096, kernel_size = 1, padding = 0, activation='relu'))
        self.net.add(nn.Conv3D(max, kernel_size = 1, activation='sigmoid'))
    def forward(self, x):
        x_c2 = self.c2(x)
        x_c3 = self.c3(x_c2)
        x_c4 = self.c4(x_c3)
        x_u5 = self.c5(x_c4)
        x4 = self.x4(x_c4) + x_u5
        x_u4 = self.u4(x4)
        x3 = self.x3(x_c3) + x_u4
        x_u3 = self.u3(x3)
        x2 = self.x2(x_c2) + x_u3
        res = self.net(x2)
        return res

Have updated the existing PR to include a ``BaseOperator`` parameter of ``render_params=False`` and tested as appropriate in ``get_template_context()``. Still trying to work out whether the natural home of this should be ``render_templates()`` or ``get_template_context()``.

For context sake, here is an example operator from one of my DAGs. The ``config`` and ``steps`` params are long YAML files which define our EMR clusters and most of these are very similar hence the need to parameterise. Some of the values passed in via the parameters are dynamic and created from other TaskInstances.

```python
create_cluster = EmrSensor(
    config='resources/emr/cluster/config.yaml',
    steps='resources/emr/steps/steps.yaml',
    params={
        'db_host': "{{ task_instance.xcom_pull(task_ids='create_db') }}",
        'year': "{{ (execution_date - macros.timedelta(days=18)).year }}",
        'month': "{{ (execution_date - macros.timedelta(days=18)).month }}"
    },
    task_id='create_cluster',
    dag=dag,
)
```

So if implemented in  `render_templates()`` are you thinking of something like this (not tested as yet)...?

```python
    def render_templates(self):
        task = self.task
        jinja_context = self.get_template_context()
        if hasattr(self, 'task') and hasattr(self.task, 'dag'):
            if self.task.dag.user_defined_macros:
                jinja_context.update(
                    self.task.dag.user_defined_macros)

        rt = self.task.render_template  # shortcut to method
        if self.render_params:
            jinja_context['params'] = rt('params', jinja_context['params'], jinja_context)

        for attr in task.__class__.template_fields:
            content = getattr(task, attr)
            if content:
                rendered_content = rt(attr, content, jinja_context)
                setattr(task, attr, rendered_content)

```

I guess it comes down to whether you view that the rendered params are special cases of normal templated variables or whether they are actually now the context for rendering - this was my original view, but after writing out what I think the updated render_templates would look like, it's a little more six of one and half a dozen of the other now. I'm happy to resubmit the PR in whichever form seems best...

NB: It seems that ``get_template_context()`` is already being called twice during the execution of ``TaskInstance.run()``. If there is a worry about performance of the templating then perhaps we should make ``render_templates()`` accept an optional context to avoid the double call, something like...

```python
        if not mark_success:
            context = self.get_template_context()

            task_copy = copy.copy(task)
            self.task = task_copy

            def signal_handler(signum, frame):
                '''Setting kill signal handler'''
                logging.error("Killing subprocess")
                task_copy.on_kill()
                raise AirflowException("Task received SIGTERM signal")
            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal_handler)

            self.render_templates(context=context)

    ...

    def render_templates(self, context=None):
        task = self.task
        jinja_context = context or self.get_template_context()
```

@tpazderka I *think* what you're proposing that mozilla-django-oidc adds a model that looks something like this:
```
class Sub2User(models.Model):
    sub = models.CharField(unique=True)
    user = models.ForeignKey('User') 
```
Then you can quickly turn the `sub` (from the claim) into a user. But it's **out of scope**. 

This issue is about finding that `User` instance in the first place. To do that there are a couple of things:

1. mozilla-django-oidc could do a better job matching by email (will is going to try to tackle the case-sensitivity part)
2. You still might have to write [your own `filter_users_by_claims`](https://mozilla-django-oidc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#connecting-oidc-user-identities-to-django-users) as Will pointed out. 

Then, the next time you could do something like this:
```
sub = claims['sub']
try:
    return Sub2User.objects.get(sub=sub).user
except Sub2User.DoesNotExist:
    user = super().filter_users_by_claims(claims)
    Sub2User.objects.create(sub=sub, user=user)
    return user
```

Either way, it's something you might want to consider in your own implementation. 

And the code above (but done nicer) could be part of your own `filter_users_by_claims` subclass method.

I've found a workaround for my ESP32 (from Makerfocus by Geekworm).

**Assumptions:**
* You've successfully flashed a Micropython firmware.
* Verify by running **picocom**
* Or verify by running a GUI term like CoolTerm for Mac. 
You should see something like
> MicroPython v1.9.1-218-g56f05137 on 2017-07-01; ESP32 module with ESP32
Type "help()" for more information.
>>>


**The Problem:**
So when I run: 
`ampy --port /dev/tty.wchusbserial1a1210 ls`

I get the following error: "could not enter raw repl":
`File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ampy/pyboard.py", line 185, in enter_raw_repl
    raise PyboardError('could not enter raw repl')`

**The Workaround:**
1. Open  /usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ampy/pyboard.py. Find line 171. Specifically go to the **enter_raw_repl** method:
`    def enter_raw_repl(self):
        self.serial.write(b'\r\x03\x03') # ctrl-C twice: interrupt any running program

        # flush input (without relying on serial.flushInput())
        n = self.serial.inWaiting()
        while n > 0:
            self.serial.read(n)
            n = self.serial.inWaiting()`
2.  Right after the **while** loop, add a **time.sleep(2**). So it becomes
`    def enter_raw_repl(self):
        self.serial.write(b'\r\x03\x03') # ctrl-C twice: interrupt any running program

        # flush input (without relying on serial.flushInput())
        n = self.serial.inWaiting()
        while n > 0:
            self.serial.read(n)
            n = self.serial.inWaiting()
        time.sleep(2)`
3. Save and rerun ampy.

You should still be able to run **ampy** with your working boards because we've only added a delay here.

**Other Notes**
1. On a good working board, this is what I see as output (I enabled console logging in the pyboard.py file):
```
$ ampy --port /dev/tty.SLAB_USBtoUART ls

O
r
>
[

boot.py

```

I just completed work to implement a solution for this internally at my company that I thought I would share. Our approach was to utilize the `on_success` trigger of Task with a method whose side effect was sending an e-mail. We too have several different pipelines running around, so we made a central function that could be applied to each task we wanted success e-mail sent when completed (typically the last task of a given pipeline) This approach only required and additional line of boilerplate to tasks that wanted success e-mail, which seemed reasonable enough. Here is a example of the code we came up with: *Using python 3.5.2 and luigi 2.3.3.* Our send e-mail function (pretty much just spews information/attributes about the completed task): ```python # Intentionally verbose and monolithic for demonstration purposes def send_success_email(task: luigi.Task) -> None: """Notifies users of completed task. Intended to be added to luigi.Task.on_success""" local_domain_name = "mycompany.com" local_smtp = ".".join(("smtp", local_domain_name)) # Our company has its own SMTP server default_email_address = ["@".join((os.environ["USERNAME"], local_domain_name))] # E-mail addresses are username@domain.com locally. Pick the user running it, if none provided luigi_config_singleton = luigi.configuration.LuigiConfigParser.instance() # Be sure to scrape the config file for information before providing a default to_addrs = luigi_config_singleton.get( "email", "receiver", default=default_email_address ).split( "," ) name_task_class = task.task_family subject_prefix = luigi_config_singleton.get( "email", "prefix", default=None, ) if subject_prefix is None: subject = list() else: subject = [subject_prefix + ":"] subject.extend([ name_task_class, "ran successfully on", os.environ["COMPUTERNAME"], ]) msg = EmailMessage() msg['Subject'] = " ".join(subject) msg['From'] = luigi_config_singleton.get( "email", "sender", default="@".join((os.environ["COMPUTERNAME"], local_domain_name)) ) msg["To"] = ",".join(to_addrs) # Prepare the body of the e-mail. Could consider more elegant HTML preparation tooling rather than somewhat messy string building # Start with a basic statement of what/when body = "

\n" body += " ".join(( name_task_class, "task finished successfully at", datetime.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S on %Y-%m-%d"), )) body += "\n

\n" # Then start mining task attributes, pick some off, and dump them to a table body += "\n" body += "\n".join([ "".format(key, value) for key, value in ( ("Task ID", task.task_id), ("Task Class Name", name_task_class), ("Computer Name", os.environ["COMPUTERNAME"]), ("User Name", os.environ["USERNAME"]), ("Parameter(s)", task.get_params()), ("Requirement(s)", task.requires()), ("Input(s)", task.input()), ("Output(s)", task.output()), ) ]) body += "\n
{}{}
\n" msg.set_content( body, subtype="html", ) # Send the e-mail with smtplib.SMTP( luigi_config_singleton.get( "smtp", "host", default=local_smtp, ), luigi_config_singleton.get( "smtp", "port", default="25", # Local default SMTP port ) ) as smtp: smtp.send_message(msg) return None ``` An example task: ```python class MyTask(luigi.Task): """An example task to demonstrate success e-mail integration design pattern""" def requires(self): """Task inputs""" pass # Define inputs here def run(self): """Do the actual task""" pass # Define the task here def output(self): """Task outputs""" pass # Define outputs here MyTask.event_handler(luigi.Event.SUCCESS)(send_success_email) # Add our e-mail function to the on_success event ``` I hope this helps!

Thanks for all the interest and discussion. I concur with @jamesls that in Boto3 you will (at least conceptually) have to specify a region. And it is easy, just use the region_name parameter.

    ow = boto3.resource('opsworks', region_name='us-east-1')
    ow = boto3.client('opsworks', region_name='us-east-1')

And the reason is:

1. "even though OpsWorks is only in a single region currently, they could eventually be available in multiple regions." (quoted from @jamesls )
2. Boto 3's API and its behavior has to be consistent across all AWS services. Region is still needed here, it is just that it happens you can only provide one value (for now).

```
echo '[profile test]' > ~/.aws/config
export AWS_DEFAULT_PROFILE=test
(your script)
```

Though keep in mind that you'll still get an error eventually if boto3 needs to make an API call that requires signing the request (which will fail because we couldn't find credentials).

Thanks for opening the PR and driving it as far as you have! I've talked with the rest of the team about this, and here's the interface we'd like to go for:

```python
from botocore.session import Session
from botocore.credentials import create_credential_resolver
from botocore.credentials import JSONFileCache


sess = Session()
cache = JSONFileCache()
resolver = create_credential_resolver(session, cache)
sess.register_component('credential_resolver', resolver)
```

There are several reasons for this. This allows a customer to specify whatever cache implementation they want instead of specifically tying them to our built-in cache implementations. We didn't simply add a `credential_cache` session component because that would only ever be used once. The way the session works, the credential chain is generated exactly once. So if you set `credential_cache` after the chain had already been generated, your new cache would never be used. We can't simply re-generate the chain because customers can provide their own, so we would effectively be overwriting their custom chain with our default chain without warning.

With this approach customers can use whatever cache they want and the process for setting it will result in the behavior they would expect out of the `Session` whenever they decide to set it. It still leaves us the ability to add config file settings later on.

I'll be taking this on unless you really want to do it yourself. Let me know what you think.

As mentioned, pyspotify has all the required pieces to add offline support to Mopidy-Spotify. However, there is no timeline on when it will be implemented and available in Mopidy-Spotify.

What is more constructive is to discuss what you expect from such a feature. I imagine a couple of ways it could work for the end user...

### Alternative A: new config `spotify/offline_playlists`

``` ini
[spotify]
offline_playlists =
  My party list
  My xmas list
  My other list
  spotify:alice:playlist:abcdef0123456789
```

#### Pros

- Easy to implement.

#### Cons

- You'll have to reconfigure and restart Mopidy every time you want to change the set of offline playlists.
- You'll have to watch the Mopidy logs to get to know anything about the status of the offline syncing.

### Alternative B: custom command to manage offline playlists

```
$ mopidy spotify offline status
My party list (spotify:...) [17/17]
My xmas list (spotify:...) [93/93]
$ mopidy spotify offline add spotify:alice:playlist:abcdef0123456789
$ mopidy spotify offline status
My party list (spotify:...) [17/17]
My xmas list (spotify:...) [93/93]
Alice's list (spotify:alice:playlist:abcdef0123456789) [1/28] [downloading: 3.6%, ~7min remaining]
```

To have this work both when Mopidy is running and not, state must be shared between the command and the Mopidy server through some on-disk persistency (e.g. JSON file or SQLite database). I imagine that all the state that is kept is what playlists are available offline and how much they have been synced, as well as a list of pending tasks (adding, removing playlists).

#### Pros

- You can add/remove offline playlists without restarting Mopidy.

#### Cons

- More to implement.
- Requires shell on the server running Mopidy to manage to offline playlists.
- I'd like to see the extension command support in Mopidy replaced with "click" before it gets wider usage.

### Alternative C: control offline playlists through Mopidy APIs

#### Pros

- Control what playlists are offline from any Mopidy client that supports the API
- Control over the network
- No need to restart Mopidy

#### Cons

- The API doesn't exist
- No clients support this

Reading all this it dawns on me that not using ContextManagers everywhere was the worst mistake that GitPython suffers from to date. It's all based on my initial observations that `__del__()` is actually called consistently when the last reference to an object is dropped.

Add the split-personality of the Python ecosystem (py2 vs py3), and one ends up in a situation where python isn't really supposed to be used for anything that doesn't yet have the massively matured and proven implementation one might need to solve the problem at hand.

It really aches me to see all this time invested just to finally have to succumb to the overall brokenness of the system. Certainly there is a fix for everything, but a rewrite might be easier after all.

So, I was looking at the code again and I think adding this feature is fairly easy. Since the API already supports environment variables, most of the heavy holding is already done. [here](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/objects/commit.py#L325,#L341). Here's what I am gonna do:

1. Add parameters to take the dates `authored_date`, `committed_date` (same as `Commit` object takes them) [here](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/objects/commit.py#L269)
2. Add a new `if` block and move rest in `elif`. Since user is specifying the dates, it takes the precedence. (explicit dates > os env > default date)
3. No need to do the parsing the date, [`parse_date`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/objects/util.py#L100) already does that.

I'm not too familiar with multiprocessing, but IIRC you can only communicate between processes using things like queues and such that are designed for the job (from the `multiprocessing` module), so the PIDController and other such things would have to be redesigned to use that. Seems like a bad idea.

I haven't looked too deeply into this since last year, so the details are hazy. If I were to go investigating this, what I probably would do is put log statements that include the time and current thread id all over the timertask code and figure out what's happening in what thread and find the race condition that's freezing it.

From the other issue:

> The PIDController uses a TimerTask object which also calls Timer.delay which increments our fake_time timer while running. Since it is running on its own thread, how many calls it makes is indeterminate and if what you wanted to get done in that time is also indeterminate."

If this is the case (which sounds plausible), then probably the right answer is to have a separate 'time' stored in a threadlocal for each thread, and to operate each thread in lockstep with each other so they can't mess with each other's timing, but also execute in a synchronized manner. For some reason I thought we did something like that, but perhaps not.

Probably at least part of the problem is that we attempt to allow 'pausing' and 'stepping' time, which makes the implementation of fake time a bit more complex.

I've been sufficiently convinced that the use case for wanting/needing full Python powers is sufficiently weird that it's not worth trying to keep it over over benefits. At this point I think it comes down to a Python-esque DSL (really just a restricted subset via AST parsing) or a static file of some kind.

So far every attempt I've seen at this comes down to a single trade off, Ease of Implementation for tool authors versus ease of use for end users. Given that a number of people have tried and this still seems to be the case, I don't think we're going to come up with some magic layout here that doesn't still fundamentally have this tradeoff.

With regards to the 3 file hting that @ncoghlan suggested. I don't think it helps much here (and in fact I think it hurts). Adding a third format increases the burden on tool authors even more since there's now a third format they have to parse and support (and very few tools will be able to omit one of them). On top of that, I think most (95%+?) people will end up standardizing around one of the two formats (whichever seems most popular most likely) and the extra work is then incurred for a tiny segment of the population.

OK, I regret beginning with "properly REST" :).  I don't put much stock in REST as a formal application architecture, and I don't really care if we are adhering to its religious tenets or not.  It's very hard to tell if this specific idea (HTML including presentation information & JSON including only structure from the same resource) is "really" REST or not.  There are some indications [that it is](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/10676577/render-output-format-html-json-xml-depending-on-parameter#10685804), but there's more consensus that [content negotiation *generally* is part of REST for deciding on different formats rather than different structures](http://blog.ploeh.dk/2015/06/22/rest-implies-content-negotiation/).

So, putting aside the idea of whether they're "really" the same concept or not: what's the benefit?  As this blogger puts it, ["less duplication of code"](http://www.yegor256.com/2014/09/09/restful-web-sites.html#what-do-we-gain).

If you're writing a web application that retrieves data from a backend, performs some logic, and returns it to a front-end, I believe it should be a _strongly encouraged default_ that you should have structured data be available on day 1, even if what you're really targeting for reasons of optimization, SEO, or user experience is a prerendered HTML page.  The goal here is not to commit to the same URL structure but rather to the same code.  The negotiation is already rather ad-hoc (a query parameter, see #132), and there are good tooling reasons to avoid a pure `Accept:`-header based negotiation strategy.  A URL segment might also be a perfectly reasonable way to accomplish the negotiation process, for example, exposing a `/api/` prefix which is always JSON and an `/app/` prefix which is always HTML.

So, all that said: what exactly do you think is the problem with offering these two resources from the same URL?  What difficulties might it present?  Would it be better to come up with some other default?

`cssselect` has only one purpose: translating a CSS3 selector to an equivalent XPath location path.

`.story-body__inner > p` is a class selector followed by a [child combinator](https://www.w3.org/TR/css3-selectors/#child-combinators) for a `p` element.

`cssselect` translates that to XPath following the [CSS3 definitions](https://www.w3.org/TR/css3-selectors/#selectors):

> E > F	: an F element child of an E element

The current implementation of `cssselect` translates this to `descendant-or-self::*[@class and contains(concat(' ', normalize-space(@class), ' '), ' story-body__inner ')]/p` which is a correct translation.

`.story-body__inner > p` should return `p` elements (and not text content from these elements), as your Chrome dev tools and Firepath show.

`lxml` is _one_ library that understands XPath. And it also selects `p` elements for the XPath translation from `cssselect` with your sample HTML document. What you do with those `p` elements is another thing. It's not `cssselect`'s job.

So no, I do not agree that this is a bug in `cssselect` that the lxml-selected `p` elements do not produce their text content using the `.text` attribute.
Feel free to open a bug on `lxml` if you want to change how to access text content from elements.
Currently, [`.text` on an element is defined as](http://lxml.de/api/lxml.etree._Element-class.html):

> text
> Text before the first subelement. This is either a string or the value None, if there was no text.

which is what you are getting.

What should we do?

> Why do you want an intermediate step?
> Do you want to isolate developers from the scrapyd server
> to introduce code reviews?

There are a few advantages that you get, for example:
* Code review, as you said, though I don't think that's actually an issue with the other deployment flow if you handle properly the merges to the production branch and such.
* Integration with other python tools, this is the most nicest one, as you can now say that for your project, you'll need the spider set (package with spiders) to be at least X version, or lower than Y, etc...
* Testability, as you can use the common widespread ways of python package testing, that is, by the version, though I agree that having the commit hash of the deployed object is a plus.
* Ease of distribution, as you can now do a release to pip (from travis, from your machine, automatically, manually...) and it will be distributed from there to any user.
* Ease of maintenance, as you can use regular python tools to actually handle and manage the spiders.

So, from my point of view, it's not really adding an extra step for the developer, as all that would be automated anyhow, it just allows using a more complex (and more widely used) package, distribution and management system.

> Also, how many scrapyd instances do you plan to run?

At first we will be running only 3 or 4 (aside from the developer own instances for testing and such), but we want to be able to scale if needed, and to do so as easily as possible.

This is an issue with the way the kubernetes API spec defines the targetPort. Even though technically it should accept either an int or a  string, the spec just flags it as a string type and therefore it is validated as a string. I think if you put the port numbers inside quotes this issue would go away.

```yaml
ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    target_port: "80"
  - name: https
    port: 443
    target_port: "443"
```

After giving it some more thought, I agree that a context manager may not be the best solution here. Also, we don't persist clients in the Python library (which is probably something we should fix too) which makes my proposed solution somewhat difficult to implement.

I don't think we should start storing responses by default however, so as not to increase the memory footprint for users who don't need this feature. Also, we should not expose any requests-specific attributes as the library can use different HTTP clients.

How about this:

- have a global configuration flag that needs to be enabled, e.g. `Stripe.include_responses = True`

- when the flag is enabled, all Stripe resources returned by the API have a `response` attribute, which is an instance of `StripeResponse`

- `StripeResponse` stores the body, headers and status code of the response

We'll also probably have to define a custom JSON encoder to exclude `response` from the serialization of Stripe objects.

Hi Paul,

Myself and many others agree with you, i.e., that there should be some code
that helps people incorporate FireWorks and pymatgen into practical tools
for running materials science workflows.

We have already released MPWorks (github.com/materialsproject/MPWorks),
which is the version of materials science workflows that is used and
developed by Materials Project. Unfortunately, it is overly complicated and
difficult to get started with. This is partly because this codebase tries
to solve very specific problems for the Materials Project itself, partly
because it was constructed in a bit of a rush, and partly because it could
have balanced simplicity against functionality better in several places.
While this code is open-source and actively developed, I would not
recommend this as a starting point for anyone.

I am personally working on an alternative code that is more along the lines
of what you are after. However, because I don't have too much time for
coding these days and my postdocs are yet to arrive, it is taking a lot
longer than expected. Thus, it might be several months before it is in any
kind of usable shape. However, I hope that within 1 year or so the issue
that you've described will no longer be a problem.

You can check the progress of this new code here:
https://github.com/hackingmaterials/matmethods

and another code that was its precursor here:
https://github.com/materialsvirtuallab/fireworks-vasp

However, I regret to say that I cannot point to a truly good solution in
the meantime as these codes are being developed.

Best,
Anubhav

p.s. If you could follow the conventions below for posting issues, it would
greatly help:

1) FireWorks issues (nothing to do with materials science):
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/fireworkflows

2) Pymatgen questions / discussion (nothing to do with workflows or
Materials Project):
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/pymatgen

3) Pymatgen *issues* (nothing to do with workflows or Materials Project):
https://github.com/materialsproject/pymatgen/issues

4) Materials project developer questions / issues (your current email fits
most neatly here):
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/matproj-develop

Hi @Page-David, thanks for the report.  I believe your understanding is correct in both cases.  

We try to expose parameters in ways which will make intuitive sense, which is likely why a variable from a paper (`k`) got changed to `scale`.  However, I would certainly approve of the suggested wording change.  As far as the default goes, I'm not sure why that specific value was chosen, but rarely is any single default appropriate in the majority of cases.  Oversegmentation is frequently desired behavior.

As for `sigma`, again the wording is intended to describe how the parameter works.  Conceptually, the width of the bell curve is controlled by `sigma`.  For those - including both you and I - who know what `sigma` means in the context of a Gaussian curve, the name of the parameter should be sufficient.  There's always a balance when it comes to making these things accessible.

Would you like to make a pull request with your suggested wording change in the `scale` parameter?

> I do not know why the value of the anomaly likelihood always begins at 0.5, I expected a starting value of 1 by the first data and will be decreased to zero...

No, this is a correct behavior. Value 0.5 means "we don't know", while 1 would mean "this is an anomaly". The code has a parametrized burn-in period (500 from top of my head) for which it always returns 0.5. After that we have enough data to start doing the statistics and can return likelihoods.

I added a third reason why we need stable URLs for communities.

I discussed with @ludmilamarian yesterday to see how CDS is handling community renaming. In fact they try to never change the URLs. When a community is renamed they just change the name displayed on the pages, not the URL. When a community is merged/split, they just close the previous community and create a new one. That way the bookmarks still work and people don't have to guess where a community disappeared.

She also mentioned that with this approach (names in URLs without renaming) another problem was appearing. The URL names at some point didn't match the new names displayed on the page. This was confusing some users.

For these reasons I would still go for a UUID in the URLs, be it from a PID store or just a field in the Community model.

@jpadilla I tested you current dev branch.
While It technically works well, it doesn't serve my needs.
Let me explain in detail:
The field I'm relying to perform user lookup is a `UUIDField`.
Following the intentation of the patch in dev branch, I define `USERNAME__FIELD = 'uuid'` on my User model.
 It means that from now on, every django app that will consume the `User` model will use `uuid` instead of `username`. The problem is that it doesn't work quite well with all the third party libraries I'm using (`python-social-auth` for instance),  because most of the time, `username` field is assumed to be a `CharField`. It means some libraries will try to create a `User` by providing `username` that is not suitable for `UUIDField`.

To recap, while it is working fine with drf-jwt, it doesn't play well with other third party libraries.

@ticosax 

When a user creates a password, it gets hashed into something like:

pbkdf2_sha256$20000$FhnlJ0rpdCli$SHBxdnk2xUMmJgok//3YaC89uVJG0eu4xz14pWAakKE=

So when when a user obtains a new JWT, you would add, say, the last 15 characters of that user's password to the token:

jwt.update({ "lucky_numbers": "eu4xz14pWAakKE=" })

And then every time you validate the JWT, you check to see if the 'lucky_numbers' field on the token matches the list 15 characters on the user's password. If not, you raise an exception, and the token is no longer valid.

This is seems like a pretty good solution because you already need to lookup a user object to both create and validate a JWT, so there wouldn't be any extra database lookups. You could also add a DateTime field on the user model called something like 'password_last_changed_at', and invalidate all tokens issued before that timestamp. However, this involves forcing the user to add an extra field to their user model, and only really helps in the one in a quadrillion case that the last 15 digits of the old password hash are the same as for the new password hash.

Before I look at the code, a few comments:

- I feel like passing different interpolants to `getLensing` (etc.) is a useful thing to be able to do, at least to be able to check that you aren't that sensitive to the choice of interpolant (because if you are, that usually means you've set up the grid in a way that is suboptimal for the problem of choice).

- I agree about deprecating `subsampleGrid` (which you haven't done yet but I thought I would comment since you mentioned it as an option).  That was mostly for testing when we were putting the basic functionality in place.

- I personally do use `calculateXi` still.  It's useful to be able to predict how much suppression of shear power you'll have due to your finite grid size.  We could deprecate some arguments while keeping the basic functionality?

In case you haven't gotten too far on this yet, I've been playing around with it while watching football.  I think this works:
```
if sys.version_info > (3,2):
    assert_warns = getattr(_t, 'assertWarns')
else:
    from contextlib import contextmanager
    import warnings
    @contextmanager
    def assert_warns(wtype, *args, **kwargs):
        if len(args) == 0:
            # When used as a context manager
            with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as w:
                warnings.simplefilter("always")
                yield w
            assert len(w) >= 1, \
                    "Calling %s did not raise a warning with %s, %s"%(f, args, kwargs)
            assert issubclass(w[0].category, wtype), \
                    "%s with %s, %s raised the wrong warning type"%(f, args, kwargs)
        else:
            # When used as a regular function
            func = args[0]
            args = args[1:]
            with assert_warns(wtype):
                res = func(*args, **kwargs)
            yield res
```
