We are publishing the xlog positions for each replica in DCS, so the monitoring system might use it to evaluate the overall cluster health (i.e. are there nodes significantly behind the master). We are not using it in Patroni to make failover-related decisions, therefore, we won't have an issue with promotion of the 'wrong' replica into master based solely on the information from xlog position from DCS.

As for the tags, we definitely want to expose them via the API, because the ultimate decision to choose a certain node as a new master would be still performed after asking the API of that node. The question is whether we also want to *change* the tags via the API (without restarting Patroni)? I think the tags like clone- and replica- from might benefit from it, as it might be necessary to disable the replica as a source for base backups if there is a problem with it, or enable it only after making sure it is up-to-date with the master and running normally.
I was using pgbackrest and ran into this problem as well. To fix it, I deleted the `initialize` key from Consul.

> Is my understanding correct ?
Yes, you want both streaming replication and WAL archiving precisely for the case when the streaming replication gets far behind (primarily due to it being down, if it cannot get connection to the master is available then the slot will not expire and the master will keep those segments). 

Also, as @CyberDem0n has already mentioned, the recovery_conf under the bootstrap/dcs section will not come into effect unless this is a brand new cluster, otherwise, use `patronictl edit-config`.

> Does patroni provide any option to delete old wal log files from archive in master?

Master should never remote anything from the WAL archive, since it only writes new files there but never restores from it. What you are looking is `archive_cleanup_command` that can be configured in the recovery.conf on replicas (https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/archive-recovery-settings.html). Be aware that it only works in the case there is only one replica, as described in the docs.

> If I understand correctly retry will fail because prevExist = False.

We are talking about network connectivity problems here.
If some other node already created initialize key it will not retry off course, but wait until master will become available to take a basebackup from it.

If we only use the ProxyFix function of the library, would it make sense to have the function in our code ?
```python
class ProxyFix(object):

    """This middleware can be applied to add HTTP proxy support to an
    application that was not designed with HTTP proxies in mind.  It
    sets `REMOTE_ADDR`, `HTTP_HOST` from `X-Forwarded` headers.  While
    Werkzeug-based applications already can use
    :py:func:`werkzeug.wsgi.get_host` to retrieve the current host even if
    behind proxy setups, this middleware can be used for applications which
    access the WSGI environment directly.
    If you have more than one proxy server in front of your app, set
    `num_proxies` accordingly.
    Do not use this middleware in non-proxy setups for security reasons.
    The original values of `REMOTE_ADDR` and `HTTP_HOST` are stored in
    the WSGI environment as `werkzeug.proxy_fix.orig_remote_addr` and
    `werkzeug.proxy_fix.orig_http_host`.
    :param app: the WSGI application
    :param num_proxies: the number of proxy servers in front of the app.
    """

    def __init__(self, app, num_proxies=1):
        self.app = app
        self.num_proxies = num_proxies

    def get_remote_addr(self, forwarded_for):
        """Selects the new remote addr from the given list of ips in
        X-Forwarded-For.  By default it picks the one that the `num_proxies`
        proxy server provides.  Before 0.9 it would always pick the first.
        .. versionadded:: 0.8
        """
        if len(forwarded_for) >= self.num_proxies:
            return forwarded_for[-self.num_proxies]

    def __call__(self, environ, start_response):
        getter = environ.get
        forwarded_proto = getter('HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PROTO', '')
        forwarded_for = getter('HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR', '').split(',')
        forwarded_host = getter('HTTP_X_FORWARDED_HOST', '')
        environ.update({
            'werkzeug.proxy_fix.orig_wsgi_url_scheme':  getter('wsgi.url_scheme'),
            'werkzeug.proxy_fix.orig_remote_addr':      getter('REMOTE_ADDR'),
            'werkzeug.proxy_fix.orig_http_host':        getter('HTTP_HOST')
        })
        forwarded_for = [x for x in [x.strip() for x in forwarded_for] if x]
        remote_addr = self.get_remote_addr(forwarded_for)
        if remote_addr is not None:
            environ['REMOTE_ADDR'] = remote_addr
        if forwarded_host:
            environ['HTTP_HOST'] = forwarded_host
        if forwarded_proto:
            environ['wsgi.url_scheme'] = forwarded_proto
        return self.app(environ, start_response)
```

I'm going to recommend `persons`. Technically (historically) the words people and persons aren't related and entered the english language at different times. `people` is supposed to be more used for a larger undetermined number of people whereas `persons` was for a smaller known number. This isn't adhered to nowadays. As `persons` fits the english pattern of adding `s` to indicate the plural of `person` I believe it's descriptively the more elegant choice.

Sorry for the delay. I have some findings and I would like to share them with you.

In my mac I first ran `virtualenv -p /usr/bin/python venv` to create the python2.7 environment. Then put my lambda function code and installed all requirements. Then I packaged everything under `venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages` along with my function as a Lambda deployment package, as Lambda official document instructed. The function can run successfully. This is because when I ran command `virtualenv` it will install `pip`, `wheels`, `setuptools` under `venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages`. And the module `pkg_resources` also shows up.

So I repeat the above step but before packaging I explicitly deleted `pkg_resources` and deployed to Lambda. Then I got a similar message "Unable to import module 'main': No module named pkg_resources".

By looking at your CI setup script I don't see any issue. After the following commands
```
virtualenv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt --quiet
```
you should have everything you need to build your Lambda deployment package.

So the question comes down to how Serverless do packaging when the framework performs packaging. I would suggest two things. First is to download your deployed function code and unzip it. Check if any of the modules installed by `virtualenv` is present. It could be that Serverless omit those modules. The second thing you could do is to add `setuptools` to your `requirements-dev.txt` and see if that module is packaged. Please let me know if that works for you.

On the other side we will look into revert the change on depending on `pkg_resources`  at runtime. It seems like both the contributor and myself overlooked to this new dependency as we take it for granted when developing under a virtual environment.

This would be ready for a review now @neubig @yoavg . I tested it with a model that takes speech inputs and get better speed and reasonable outputs. I also tried the static-graph example from the documentation to make sure I didn't break anything there. I tried to keep API changes at a minimum, but this PR will break existing code that uses static graphs and inputTensor (in order to unbreak, people will have to add ```reusable_expr=True``` as argument). Alternatively, we could make True the default, but that will limit the speed-up experienced by users unless they add ```reusable_expr=False``` in their code.

Changes are summarized as follows:
- avoid some unnecessary intermediate calls
- for flat inputs, skip call to np.flatten()
- for non-reusable inputs, directly use float-vectors rather than the rather slow FloatVectorValue container
- best speed is achieved by the new inputTensorTranspose() operator that takes in a flat numpy array and desired dimensions and will directly pass it through in column-major format.
- deactivate bound checks

As before, we make sure things are not garbage-collected prematurely by referencing the stored values in the returned Expression which is itself held in the computation graph. I have a feeling that more speed-up might be possible, but this is about as far as I got without spending too much time digging into Cython memory management.

Transparent reading of gzipped (and bzip2) files including NEXRAD Level 2 files is supported by `pyart.io.read` but not the underlying `read_*` functions.  So the following just works:

```
radar = pyart.io.read('KTLX20110524_000032_V3.gz')
```

Peeking at the beginning of the file is used to detected gzipped files in the auto_read module which is a  much more robust and reliable method then checking if the filename ends with .gz which can be error prone and does not work with is a file like object is passed to NEXRADLevel2File.  I think this method could be abstracted out and used in other functions and classes. > Imported names should always be considered an implementation detail. >>> import subprocess

2. I think that the dataset was a good higher-level concept, but it kinda got bulldozed when the major restructuring happened. You basically already have a `dataset` module, which is `api`. I think it's better to construct a `dataset`  from `datastore` and leave `datastore` for meta-methods like `set_defaults()` now. 

3. I really really don't think you should be adding functionality to `Entity`. It inevitably ends up moving towards ORM which inevitably makes the library more and more heavyweight, and duplicates use-cases from NDB and DB. If anything I think the easiest way forward is to *simplify* entity. The most lightweight syntax I can think of is:

SETUP
```
datastore.set_defaults() //does auth and connection stuff
d = datastore.Dataset('dataset-id', **kwargs) //more advanced options like read_options should be put here since it is a one time operation
```
Put a single entity
```
key = d.put(Key(*path), Entity(**props))
```
Put a bunch of entities
```
d.put({key1: entity1, key2: entity2 ...})
```
Get an entity
```
d.get(Key(*path))
```

`rollback` exists to allow the *application* to signal the back-end that it will never call `commit`, and therefore that the back-end can release any resources for that transaction.

I cannot see any possible way to reuse the opaque `transaction` field (returned in `beginTransaction`) after `commit` API using it fails.  The back-end folks will have torn down / released anything they allocated in `beginTransaction` when the failed the `commit`, and don't need us to tell them to do so by calling `rollback` (in fact, I would bet that `rollback` after a failed `commit` would error out).

Folders, per se, don't exist in Google Cloud Storage.  Per the [Object Naming API doc](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/naming#object-considerations):

> Object names reside in a flat namespace within a bucket, which means that different buckets can have objects with the same name. It also means that objects do not reside within subdirectories in a bucket. For example, you can name an object `/europe/france/paris.jpg` to make it appear that paris.jpg resides in the subdirectory `/europe/france`, but to Cloud Storage, the object simply exists in the bucket and has the name `/europe/france/paris.jpg`.

The console web UI *synthesizes* folders based on those names, but there is no actual folder to be listed.

Broken by the move of the actual module to `spanner_v1.client` (Sphinx wants the real module, not just any alias).  See 8bd57c5 (PR #5764).

The spanner docs distinguish between "usage" pages (narrative, with examples). vs "API reference" pages (autogen from docstrings/signatures).  The API reference pages are all linked on the [`api-reference` sub-index](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/spanner/api-reference.html)

> Also, https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/spanner/session-api.html may also be a good thing to add to the navbar as well.

The back-end team specifically requested suppressing mentions of direct session API usage:  they want users to invoke session-related stuff via the higher-level `database.snapshot` and `database.run_in_transaction` helpers.

regarding parallelism, I'm leaning towards this rough sketch for the case of all existing `ConsoleTask` type impls:

  1) each `@console_rule` gets a `Console`/`Terminal` type object instance injected.
  2) those rules execute and can perhaps `console.std{out,err}.write()` to a unique virtual console (and maybe those are full file-like objects. unclear how they're backed exactly). (`yield` could work here too, but would not give us multiple streams - maybe that doesn't matter tho. or maybe we could combine them into a `yield console.stdout.write('blah')` to yield structured output with stream info, etc.)
  3) if there's exactly 1, that becomes the main console
  4) if there are multiple, we batch and emit output as they complete - and:
     A) if we're running under a tty, we can provide a curses virtual console UI feature to be able to jump to any one of the given virtual consoles to see realtime rendering per console. we could also somehow highlight which consoles were getting active output via the curses interface, or auto-jump to them.
     B) otherwise, if we're piped somewhere or running in CI, we emit buffered output as each completes (with some kind of periodic status display showing whats outstanding, ala iron/scoot).
     C) an option to emit interleaved lines as they're rendered might also be useful, but off by default probably or maybe a special VC.

if something needs interactivity (e.g. `repl` or interactive cases of `run`), I think that's a slightly different variant (`@interactive_console_rule`?) with different behavior - namely, serialization/locking for tty device access in the default mode such that only one interactive thing is executing at once. we could also potentially address parallel interactivity via the virtual console impl via pseudo-terminals like screen does, but that could get tricky. running only one interactive rule at once seems like a good first start tho.

another interesting idea might be to port `./pants server` to the daemon and then hotlink/auto-open a view from the console to a web interface that could provide a web view of the virtual consoles (think travis/jenkins live output etc).

one possible further idea for #2 might involve live streaming `@console_rule` output via the nailgun protocol as numbered streams beyond 0, 1, 2 and then let the client-side handle rendering those to VCs, interspersing or whatever other configurable display modes we can come up with.

other stray thoughts:

> The big, big sticking point is that in order for any of the requests the task makes to be memoized, it would need to be running pre-fork.

or to be able to compute those requests pre-fork somehow, such that we could warm them pre-fork and then re-request post-fork cheaply? It's not immediately clear to me what sort of dynamic product requests one of these sorts of rules would be making that couldn't be computed pre-fork or via a regular, underlying `@rule`?

> Unless there is exactly one running maybe? Yea, I think that makes sense. Because certainly we're going to have some other UX for the progress of @rule execution, so not rendering a frontend task's output until it completes would be plenty reasonable.

this makes perfect sense to me and I think it'll all come down to the UI/UX. I think there's also a question of what sort of logic the `@console_rule` would fully encapsulate. would it be a full blown e.g. `Task` impl, or just a display driver that's accumulating finer grained products and simply displaying those? I'm sort of thinking the latter, but it'd be interesting to sketch this out end to end for the python tasks. for example, we'd discussed the idea of executing `py.test` and collecting structured output for that in sub-`@rule`s, then aggregating all of that in a `@console_rule`. if we think of `@console_rule` as more of a display driver here, then it's logic could intrinsically handle optimal output interleaving of structured inputs. this could mean that a single "test executor" `@console_rule` could potentially drive parallel junit and pytest test execution as sub-`@rule`s and then optimally interleave that even for disparate test runners.

> how are frontend rules selected/installed, and what subject types are available to them to kick off execution?

they're probably installed like any other rule + some `register.py`-esque goal->rule mapping? likely with a v2 `GoalRunner`-esque driver that understands the mappings and would synchronously call into the engine to request/excute them?

from there, kickoff subject types are probably options, targetroots, ?

> would not be "just python code":
> what does access to the filesystem look like?

trying to understand a concrete case where these would need real access to the filesystem vs the ability to operate in a sandboxed snapshot as subprocess - or just delegating that to a sub-`@rule`? but in general, unfettered filesystem access is probably a non-starter for any variant of `rule`? would be good to talk through this a bit tho.

> what do access to stderr/stdout/ttys look like?

I think we virtualize stderr/stdout, for sure. ttys are trickier, particularly if we need to hand a real tty to a subprocess like in the case of v1 repls. thinking that initially it'd probably be easiest to just synchronize access to the main tty for any 'interactive' thing, then make everything else non-interactive by default.

we'd also talked about the idea of directly `execv()`'ing a built product that e.g. had a repl entrypoint post-run, which might ultimately be the cleanest way to handle any kind of task interactivity?

> what would the UX for the @rules powering a frontend rule look like, given that we'd be giving frontend rules IO access while the @rules powering them might still be running

I think all of the console access here by rules is fully virtualized, so we'd ultimately have control over how to display process in the UI/UX impl. there are many console UI techniques we could employ here, and thinking about the potential there is making me pretty antsy to begin work on that :)

There is an ivy resolver-specific setting (on by default for some
resolvers) that will cause ivy itself to copy or symlink from the directory
where the artifact is found. This is required in pants for sanity in the
presence of the remote buildcache.

useOrigin=true

See:
http://ant.apache.org/ivy/history/2.1.0/use/retrieve.html

BootstrapJvmTools extends IvyTaskMixin and IvyTaskMixin writes cached classpaths to a cache. But there is a problem with remote cache because a classpath.raw file from Ivy contains absolute paths and it won't work on another machine.

I think we can download a shaded tool from a remote cache instead of shading it locally. It will save around a minute for a cold Scala/Java compile because shading Zinc takes ~1 min.

seems like this relates to the passing of both `globals` and `locals` to `exec()`.

the fix is to avoid passing `locals` as an empty dict and letting the default behavior takeover where the scope binding just works:

```
>>> BUILD = '''
... A_CONST = '123'
... def getter():
...   return [A_CONST]
... target(dependencies=[target2(dependencies=getter())])
... '''
>>> def parse_fix(build_file):
...   t, t2 = Registrar(), Registrar()
...   exec(build_file, {'target': t, 'target2': t2})
... 
>>> parse_fix(BUILD)
>>> 
```

Thanks for brining this up! Sockeye is indeed unaware of CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES and assumes the device ids are the absolute ids. In case you know the exact device id you want to use I'd either disable locking via `--device 0 --disable-device-locking` (as you suggested) or directly specify the device id you want to use without using `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` or have `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` set to the all devices. To some extend `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` fulfills the same purpose as device locking in Sockeye, namely to make sure no two trainings run on the same device.

Maybe for the time being we could add a warning if locking is enabled and `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` is set. For the longer term we could think about adding the parsing/mapping logic based on `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` as you suggested.

I sort of worked around this from the other direction. We use a Swagger spec with the Amazon extensions for our deployment, and then we run a command to pull the "clean" Swagger spec back from API Gateway:

```
#!/bin/bash

ApiStack=MyApiStack
ApiStageName=Api
SwaggerFile=api-swagger.json

ApiId=`aws cloudformation describe-stacks --stack-name $ApiStack | jq -r '.Stacks[0].Outputs[] | select(.OutputKey == "ApiId") | .OutputValue'`

aws apigateway get-export --rest-api-id ${ApiId} --stage-name ${ApiStageName} --export-type swagger ${SwaggerFile}
```

(Note that this requires your API stack to have ApiId, referencing the AWS::Serverless::Api resource, as one of its outputs.)

@pdellaert 
I added a commit to fix support for multiple sessions.

You should always call start() on every session you want to use in the script before using a with statement. Following snippet works both using Python 2.7 and 3:

    session1 = vsdk.NUVSDSession( username=username, password=password, 
            enterprise=enterprise, api_url="https://10.30.31.73:8443")
    session2 = vsdk.NUVSDSession( username=username, password=password, 
            enterprise=enterprise, api_url="https://10.30.31.60:8443")

    session1.start()
    session2.start()

    with session1:
        ent = vsdk.NUEnterprise(name="p3-Level1")
        session1.user.create_child(ent)
        
        with session2:
            ent = vsdk.NUEnterprise(name="p3-Level2")
            session2.user.create_child(ent)
        
            with session1:
                ent = vsdk.NUEnterprise(name="p3-Level3")
                session1.user.create_child(ent)
        
                with session2:
                    ent = vsdk.NUEnterprise(name="p3-Level4")
                    session2.user.create_child(ent)

Outside a with statement, vspk will use the last session start() was called on

Hallo @dundeemt 

First of all, really nice work. I especially enjoy the generous amount of integration tests \o/ (which really are functional tests, I will have to rename this soon).

I left some inline comments. Forgive the number of them, I tend to be a bit nit-picky. If you disagree with any of them, just let me know so we can discuss.

A few general thoughts came up while reviewing.

As you may have noticed, I was rambling on and on about whet the Sonos devices actually do, when you hand them a list of arguments. If it forms a single query to its internal database or executes them one by one. The more I read on, it seems as if you have actually concluded, that it executes them one by one. If I am correct in this assumption, then:

 1. I think it is all the more prudent to collect interable inputs into a single argument and always make just the one request, as that is what will be done on the other side anyway and it will have a (potentially large) performance impact.
 2. I think this one-by-one execution mode **and** its implications for deletions **and** the fact that it happens whether the user suppliers lists or strs should be clearly highlighted in the docstring, maybe with a Note or Warning box.

With regards to @lawrenceakka comments about using indexing. While I agree that it maybe could create a nicer interface, I still think we should also have the normal methods as implemented here. So that in the future, one would be able to to either or.

I'm a bit confused by the various webpack config files in the root directory:

* `parse_bundle_plugin.js` and `karma.conf.js` reference `webpack.config.base.js`
* `webpack.config.dev.js` references `webpack.config.js`
* `webpack.config.base.js` and `webpack.config.js` _do not_ reference each other

Seems like perhaps both `webpack.config.base.js` and `webpack.config.js` are trying to play the "base" role?

> It's also not clear what happens in the UI if the same content resource exists in multiple channels.

Topics and resources are identified in the URL by "node id", which should be globally unique. The same resource in two places will have the same "content id", but different "node ids". So that shouldn't be a problem here.

This is executed in the same worker ( actually in the same process ) but after the task function has returned, so I think it meets our needs. So I would propose that we add a new register_XXX(...) function [here](https://github.com/girder/girder_worker/blob/master/girder_worker/core/__init__.py), to allow executors to register cleanup code ( we could keep the concept generic if we want, may be after_run ). Then we could change the task definition [here](https://github.com/girder/girder_worker/blob/master/girder_worker/tasks.py#L6) to something like this

```python
@app.task(name='girder_worker.run', after_return=cleanup)
```

Then we would implement the cleanup function so that it would look to see if the executor has a cleanup callback registered and call it if necessary. Then in the docker plugin we could just need to register our GC code and everything should work out. Does that make sense? Here is an example of a service announcing using https://github.com/jstasiak/python-zeroconf:

```python
from zeroconf import ServiceInfo, Zeroconf

class WebSocketManager(service.Service, object):
    ws_service_name = 'Verasonics WebSocket'
    wsPort = None
    wsInfo = None

    def __init__(self, factory, portCallback):
        factory.protocol = BroadcastServerProtocol
        self.factory = factory
        self.portCallback = portCallback
        self.zeroconf = Zeroconf()

    def privilegedStartService(self):
        self.wsPort = reactor.listenTCP(0, self.factory)
        port = self.wsPort.getHost().port

        fqdn = socket.gethostname()
        ip_addr = socket.gethostbyname(fqdn)
        hostname = fqdn.split('.')[0]

        wsDesc = {'service': 'Verasonics Frame', 'version': '1.0.0'}
        self.wsInfo = ServiceInfo('_verasonics-ws._tcp.local.',
                                  hostname + ' ' + self.ws_service_name + '._verasonics-ws._tcp.local.',
                                  socket.inet_aton(ip_addr), port, 0, 0,
                                  wsDesc, hostname + '.local.')
        self.zeroconf.register_service(self.wsInfo)
        self.portCallback(port)

        return super(WebSocketManager, self).privilegedStartService()

    def stopService(self):
        self.zeroconf.unregister_service(self.wsInfo)

        self.wsPort.stopListening()
        return super(WebSocketManager , self).stopService()
```

Btw, in case you wonder about the answer to [this question](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/mqtt/XFVsEIaMB6M): "MQTT - QoS - what does TCP not provide which MQTT does?"

I think this is a question that naturally arises, and the question is formulated nicely.

The answer is in the spec:

> 4.4 Message delivery retry

> When a **Client reconnects** with CleanSession set to 0, both the Client and Server MUST re-send any unacknowledged PUBLISH Packets (where QoS > 0) and PUBREL Packets using their original Packet Identifiers [MQTT-4.4.0-1]. **This is the only circumstance where a Client or Server is REQUIRED to redeliver messages.**

and here

> Non normative comment
> Historically retransmission of Control Packets was required to overcome data loss on some older TCP networks. This might remain a concern where MQTT 3.1.1 implementations are to be deployed in such environments.

---

That means, we don't need any timers or such (because TCP takes care). It also means: without session resumption, bot QoS 1 and 2 are void.

I know it's a bit hidden, but in the [Release Notes of version 1.4.0](https://github.com/swisscom/cleanerversion/releases/tag/v1.4.0) it says that the DB schema is changed from that version on. Concretely, this means that column names are forced to be lowercase in versions >= 1.4.0. So, if you set up your schema with a version <1.4.0 (where column names were CamelCase combined with underlines) this may be the error. 
Unfortunately, we didn't write any migrating script for this. 

Looking at your stacktrace, this may be the reason for the error you're getting. 
In order to be sure, it's not something else, try to run the unit tests that come with the CleanerVersion sources.
If you already are sure, do the following steps to resolve (assuming my guess was right):
- back up your DB
- change the column names to be lowercase (simply replacing uppercase letters by lowercase ones should do the trick)
- test

The easiest thing of course would be to throw away the current DB and to let Django create a new one. But this also means that your data will be gone. ;)

Looking forward to your feedback.
-Manuel

It's also pretty easy to "monkey patch" a Python object with a custom equality function by overriding the `__eq__` method on an object:

```
In [1]: class Chord(object):
   ...:     pass
   ...:

In [2]: x = Chord()

In [3]: y = Chord()

In [4]: x == y
Out[4]: False

In [5]: def __eq__(self, other):
   ...:     return True
   ...:

In [6]: Chord.__eq__ = __eq__

In [7]: x == y
Out[7]: True
```

In this case, the two objects will always be equal since my custom equality object always returns `True`. If you want to just compare pitches in a Chord you can define the parameters for equality and it will work as you expect.

I think Myke's right on this one, though -- just because two chords share the same pitch content doesn't make them the same.

This appears to me as a bioformats issue. Bioformats recognizes the image shape and sorts out the axes. As it appears, you have a CTYX image. The 'series' axis is meant for images that consists of multiple measurements, for instant at different positions or even with different techniques. You have size 27 separate measurements in your file.

I had these issues before with CTZYX .nd2 images, T and Z dimensions are flipped or put into the series channel. This was solved in a later bioformats version. Do you use the default version that is automatically downloaded by pims?

You might want to try out https://github.com/soft-matter/pims_nd2 , this circumvents bioformats.

> I am [...] not familiar with unit and integration tests.

I spent some time looking around for good articles, and to be honest [this Stack Overflow answer about the different types of tests](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4904096/whats-the-difference-between-unit-functional-acceptance-and-integration-test) sums things up nicely.

Within PyISO we define unit tests and integration tests as roughly:

- **Unit Test:** They test individual functions or client methods. Notably:
   1. They have no external dependencies (i.e. they make no network calls). Given that our project makes so many network requests to ISO and balancing authority web sites for electricity data, that means we frequently make use of the [mock](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/unittest.mock.html) library to mock out expected responses from network calls rather than making the network calls. The expected responses are stored in a `fixtures` directory in the `tests` folder.
   2. They are run by the continuous integration server ([Travis CI](https://travis-ci.org/WattTime/pyiso)) when a pull request is opened. You'll see the status reflected in your pull request. Generally, all tests must pass before we merge pull requests into the `master` branch.
   3. They're expected to be non-random in nature.
- **Integration Test:** They check integration between PyISO modules and with the servers of ISOs and balancing authorities we use as data providers. Notably:
   1. These tests make network calls and work with real data.
   2. Some tests require that environment variables on the host machine are set. Fore example, ISONE requires a username/password to retrieve data, which we set as environment variables.
   3. Dates are not fixed. They should test current or recent time ranges. This helps us catch problems if our external data providers change something about their data.

Directories containing unit tests and integration tests, structured as follows:
```
pyiso
  + --- tests
  |       + --- unit
  |       |         | test_base.py
  |       |         | test_client_one.py
  |       |         | test_client_two.py
  |       |
  |       + --- integration 
  |                 | integration_test_client_one.py
  |                 | integration_test_client_two.py
  |       
  + --- pyiso
          | base.py
          | client_one.py
          | client_two.py
```

You can fix this by either
A) Using the IP addresses directly (obviously this is not as HA as you would like it)
B) Use a DNS provider that can emulate (some of) the CNAME behaviour on top level domains.

I'm running this on a cluster as-is: `$arg_{{ $affinityArg }}` is definitely intended.

$arg_*whatever* an [embedded variable](http://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_core_module.html#variables) announced in nginx 0.7.8 that allows us to extract a specific var from the query string.

In my case, the query string `a` represents random id generated by a javascript client which persists until refresh, window close, or any other javascript-stopper.

The nginx team stole the `hash $var consistent;` directive from [libketama](https://github.com/RJ/ketama). As long as all upstreams are reachable from all routers, it won't matter which router the request passes through. Once you start thinking about failure modes though, it all goes haywire. I'm running microflow to nginx as a precaution since my entire stack is wildly experimental.

But libketama is designed for minimal bucket reassignment in case of upstream failure: only those buckets served by the now missing upstream will be reassigned.

@kalefranz here is another important one where I believe there would be high value for minimal time commitment.  Basically the idea would be:

* install time:
    * unpack conda package into package cache
    * `find /path/to/conda/package/dir/in/cache -type f | chmod a-w` to make all files read-only
* remove time:
    * `find /path/to/conda/package/dir/in/cache -type f | chmod u+w` to make all files writable (so they can be deleted)
    * `rm -Rf /path/to/conda/package/dir/in/cache`

Linking into environments could be left unchanged: they'd simply link in the read-only versions from the package cache.  And directories would still be writeable.

This is particularly important for #1329 (better support for shared conda envs in multi-user systems), which is a serious situation for our commercial adoption/deployment of Anaconda.

Again, I'm fine if this is an option rather than the default, but I strongly believe we need this both from the CLI and from `.condarc` (or wherever Conda configuration is living these days) -- the latter so it can be made the default on a case-by-case basis (e.g. "for me, I always want this behavior" or "multi-user conda environments, I want extra safety for my users that they can't write to files")

@bkreider do you have any more recent (last 2 years) perspectives on this?

@mcg1969 does it seem like this would impact any of the work you've done on package management and package cache management?

Sorry to spam others, but also interested in input on this issue from @msarahan and @groutr .
I agree that the behavior you're describing is a bug. `conda` is *supposed* to add an implicit constraint to `python` to prevent it from being upgraded to Python 3 unless explicitly requested.

Pretty much exactly what you propose with a convention for python function names would be the natural choice. However, there could come a time in the not-so-distant future where we start to transition conda to a compiled language. So in that case we don’t necessarily have the assumption that a python interpreter is available.

I’m still struggling here to think through what’s best both mid- and long-term.

Sorry guys -- was out yesterday and am just getting back to this.

*Note: I'm using "package" to refer to the concept of a package -- it's means to be abstract.  If you see `Package`, it refers to the object created in this PR.

To echo @pzwang's comments, I'm not sure I follow @teoliphant.  Let's set aside that `Package` is defined as an object and looking at it more abstractly, I'm not sure I see a reason to dislike creating a data structure that can concretely be called a package.  Encapsulation of this sort along with testing of those data structures is what allows a programmer to know that a structure they can reason about it in certain ways.  In Python, I know that I can do `"foo" in some_dict` when I have a dictionary; that I can slice lists when I have a list; and in this case that a `Package` always has an array of dependencies.  The plan is to take that further to define explicitly the behavior and data structures relevant to a `Package`, but those can be added as needed.

Currently, there is no defined data structure that represents a package.  The closest thing is that we assume that `index.json` files in a certain place have certain characteristics, so if we load them we have something we can infer is a package.  The original issue I opened showed a case where that data was incorrect which lead to the issue that @ilanschnell patched.  Which brings me to that patch.

ccb98040fced94256c7a7f2a6a2cd07dff62eabd introduces another issue: now there are two different versions of a package inside conda.  There are the five different places where an `index.json` file is opened and loaded via `json.load`.  Four of those code paths are going to have `depends` based on what's in the file.  One, however, will always have `depends`.  This separate code path means that we can't verify that a package really is a package without checking first.

This PR does *not* solve that problem yet, but it's meant to serve as a foundation to build toward removing that code duplication.  In attempting to strategically refactor, my goal isn't to solve each problem entirely on the first go.  Instead, I'm focusing on issues as they arise and addressing the underlying issue.  @ilanschnell is correct, ccb98040fced94256c7a7f2a6a2cd07dff62eabd solves the explicit issue that was caused in one explicit code path, but at the expense additional technical debt (we have to keep track of which code path the package information comes from to know what information it contains).  This PR strikes at the root cause -- the lack of a custom data structure that is verifiably a package.

Here is a patch that seems to fix the `conda install --revision` bug for conda 4.3.24.
The `dists` variable is looped over twice in the `ensure_linked_actions`, but if it were a generator the second loop is empty.  This fix turns it to a tuple which iterates in the same way every time.

```patch
diff --git a/conda/plan.py b/conda/plan.py
index 306eaf61..b5bdb08b 100644
--- a/conda/plan.py
+++ b/conda/plan.py
@@ -805,7 +805,7 @@ def revert_actions(prefix, revision=-1, index=None):
     if state == curr:
         return {}
 
-    dists = (Dist(s) for s in state)
+    dists = tuple(Dist(s) for s in state)
     actions = ensure_linked_actions(dists, prefix)
     for dist in curr - state:
         add_unlink(actions, Dist(dist))
```

I got bitten pretty badly today by some changes I made to my bootstrap scripts to accommodate for https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/3175

The problem was that `$CONDA_PREFIX/bin` was on my `PATH` (the accommodation) so I did not have build environment isolation so the `conda` `gcc` package I had installed into my root environment was being used instead of the system's GCC. The previous behaviour of activating the build environment would have resulted in the root environment being scrubbed from `PATH` giving us environmental isolation.

This meant my builds were all wrong since if you use conda's `gcc` you also need to use conda's `libgcc` (I want to use neither, but this is just one example of how this change causes the wrong stuff to leak into builds).

This prevents anything approaching reproducible builds and I must again object to this change in behaviour. If some shells cannot be supported with the old way of activating then IMHO we should not compromise the activation procedure and environment under which `conda/conda-build` operate by attempting to bend over backwards to support them.

I have taken a quick look at the code, and it seems that the `csh/tcsh` code-path requires `bash` anyway (and also `awk` and `ps`). I expect that people who use shells other than `bash` may attempt to not even have that installed. I also think that the assumption that `awk` and `ps` are installed (and specific versions of them too, with more bending over to accommodate a plurality of versions *) is not ideal.

It seems to me that all this complication could all be done away with by providing `activate.tcsh` and `deactivate.tcsh` scripts that use ($_) since `tcsh` seems to be the only shell that does not provide the minimum amount of compatibility/emulation with/of `bash` that would be required to launch a shell-specific script (on its own, without the need of `bash` and `awk` and `ps`). If other shells are similarly brain damaged then they need custom scripts too.

* a relatively minor point, I don't get the `Cygwin/mingw-w64/MSYS2` `ps` distinctions. They all use the same `ps` executable; or are you trying to support the old `mingw.org/msys` software?

This looks good, thanks.  Unfortunately c-blosc only supports being called effectively from multi-threaded operations in version >= 1.5, and with lesser versions, c-blosc makes sure that a lock is put during multi-threaded operation.

Also, and due to some not completely clear reasons, c-blosc 1.5 series still have some stability problems with bcolz (e.g. https://github.com/Blosc/bcolz/issues/120, https://github.com/Blosc/bcolz/issues/121), and this is why bcolz still comes with c-blosc 1.4 series.  The result is that you probably won't be seeing any speedup with this PR (yet, and until c-blosc 1.5 could go into bcolz).

At any rate, if you can compile bcolz with c-blosc 1.5 and tell us the speed-ups that you are seeing, that would be great.  I am a big fan of seeing real improvements before adding new code.

Currently you can not run aliases through eAPI using pyeapi. Your screenshot example looks like it would work with the autoComplete parameter and you can use this now with pyeapi by sending autoComplete=True to the execute method of the EapiConnection object.

```
import pyeapi
node = pyeapi.connect(username=[user], password=[password], host=[host])
output = node.execute(['sh ver'], autoComplete=True)
```

I'll work on adding the support for autoComplete with the run_commands method in addition to adding support for the expandAliases parameter. If you need to run using the expandAlias parameter before I have the updates in pyeapi you can use the jsonrpclib library in the below format.

```
from jsonrpclib import Server
node = Server('https://[user]:[password]@[host]/command-api')
output = node.runCmds(version=1, cmds=['ALIASCOMMAND'], format='json', expandAliases=True)
```

The way I see it the benefit has to outweigh the cost and complexity so lets analyse them.

Benefits:
- **Reduce image size**: Avoiding to install Python in the simulator docker image which saved 25% or 100 MByte in your case.
  1. However with commercial simulators the relative saving is less since these installs are often > 1 GByte.

Costs:
- **Increased complexity**: VUnit path handling needs to be abstracted and VUnit internal codebase needs to consider a mapping between host/container in many places. It would require a lot of unit tests. It is not enough to do this just in the simulator layer of VUnit even though adding a new simulator is quite easy. Paths are added to generics by VUnit which is sent to the simulator layer, those paths have to be substituted before they become string generics. 
- **Leaky abstraction**: The user has to do path substitution in the run.py file for the things which VUnit cannot know is a path.
  1. The user can set a path via a generic and VUnit has no way of knowing that it was a path and not some other string which would inhibit safe replacement.
  2. The user can set compile_option or sim_option which depending on simulator could include a path. VUnit has no way of knowing that it is a path and cannot replace it with 100% certainty. 

In this cost/benefit analysis I think the costs outweigh the benefit for VUnit. The way I see it 100 Mb larger docker image (25% saving in the best case scenario)  is not worth increased complexity in the VUnit code base and presenting a leaky abstraction to the user. To me just installing Python in the image and get everything working without issues looks more attractive. 

At work we have docker images for simulation using a commercial simulator and the size of Python in those are not significant. The docker image for the simulator is built on top of the docker image with Python which is already needed for the slave machines to have a controlled environment. In that way the Python layer is already cached on the slaves and the simulator layer is the only thing that needs to be downloaded again (Often that is cached as well).

Thanks for the question and showing me that duplicate.  It's pretty funny how someone else was building the same thing as me at around the same time.  Nice work on elasticsearch-py, and thanks for your interest in adding this connection class.

In the link I provided it shows the supported "request" libraries in app engine.  urllib2 is supported unfortunately urllib3 is not.

I did get python-requests and elasticsearch-py working on app engine, but requests is a new feature and I needed to import a lot of libraries and add some configuration to our project for just this one library.  

So I decided to make a urlfetch connection class.  This gives us a more reliable and automatic (no import+configuration+testing) option.  All the non-urlfetch libraries are converted to urlfetch in the end so it also has a performance improvement.  

In summary URLFetchHttpConnection saves app engine users time, reduces application complexity, improves performance, and is more supported than the proxy libraries.

I've worked on a couple of multiprocessing approaches. The simple way is to replace `Thread` with `multiprocessing.Process` and reuse the rest of `jobs.py` task queueing infrastructure. The quick hack sort of works, but I think we now have to work on sharing the queue structure between processes.

I'm now pursuing a second approach using `multiprocessing.Pool` to do the queuing. That will replace about 80% of `jobs.py`. I have [some sample code](https://gist.github.com/NelsonMinar/e11eeb524b4940a40ddf#file-mp-py) that includes using SIGALRM to set a total per-job timeout to interrupt runaways. It seems to work, tomorrow I'll see if I can shoehorn this approach into our code.

So a couple of information, regardless of whether you run a localhost server or a remote one we always _strongly suggest_ setting up https/SSL and password. 

This is in [our docs](http://jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.io/en/latest/public_server.html#securing-a-notebook-server) and we are enforcing that a little more each time we make a new major release. We also provide [a tool ](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/tools/secure_notebook.py) to automaticlly secure notebooks. 

As far as I can tell, once a notebook server is secured over https + password the server should be immune to the attach describe.  

If they are over http, then notebook are anyway susceptible to another wide range of attack. Maybe not remote with DNS poisoning, but they give all users and program of the current machine shell access as the user who run the notebook servers/kernels. 

Unfortunately it is relatively hard to automatically generate a trusted-self-signed certificate for a machine it is (still) something we cannot enforce without manual user intervention.

@bintoro,
first, I think store errors on the model object instead of raise a Exception is really a better design.
Exception is more rough, and will break normal flow. And I found I would like not to raise Exception at most situation. for example:

1) a rest-api for user to update his sex, but the client give the wrong value. I would not return a 500 error, but raise  400 bad-request.
```
# exception
try:
     models.validate()
except Exception as e:
     raise Exception('http 400 %s' % e.message)

# not raise 
if not models.validate():
      raise Exception('http 400 %s' % models.errors)
```
the later is more clean.

2) some times, after validate() errors, I may want to log the error msg and do some clean. but the only way to get the error msg is catch the exception. and I have to do log and clean on the except block.

3) if really need, I would like raise Exception myself, I may raise Http400, http401, http500 and so on. but the code raise validate-exception, which usually case a http500 errors. I may need catch a exception and reraise another exception.

Based on @j-towns's autograd code, I think this is a way to get forward-mode for free in TensorFlow:

```python
import numpy as np
import numpy.random as npr
import tensorflow as tf

def fwd_gradients(ys, xs, d_xs):
  """Forward-mode pushforward analogous to the pullback defined by tf.gradients.
  With tf.gradients, grad_ys is the vector being pulled back, and here d_xs is
  the vector being pushed forward."""
  v = tf.placeholder(ys.dtype, shape=ys.get_shape())  # dummy variable
  g = tf.gradients(ys, xs, grad_ys=v)
  return tf.gradients(g, v, grad_ys=d_xs)

A = tf.constant(npr.randn(5, 3), dtype=tf.float32)
x = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [1, 5])
y = tf.tanh(tf.matmul(x, A))
u = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [1, 5])

jvp = fwd_gradients(y, x, u)

x_val = npr.randn(1, 5)
u_val = npr.randn(1, 5)

init_op = tf.initialize_all_variables()
with tf.Session() as sess:
  sess.run(init_op)
  print sess.run(jvp,  feed_dict={x: x_val, u: u_val})
```

It does extra work ahead of time (i.e. at graph construction time), but those extra graph nodes being built shouldn't be involved at runtime, and the TF executor should in principle be able to carry out the Jacobian-vector product without any FLOP or memory overhead compared to a manual implementation of forward-mode. I haven't studied it closely, though, so there could be some subtleties or drawbacks.

After talking with @jvivian  and @hannes-ucsc , I think a better solution is to batch samples  based on the number of VCFs that can comfortably fit on a single node. I am working on a job function that reserves all cores for a node (to prevent other jobs from running and taking up disk space), queries the free disk space, and finally batches the GVCFs such that the batches fit on the node and are approximately the same size. There is the problem of having a batch that is too small for VQSR, so we should also check that there is a minimum number of variants per batch. GATK recommends around 25,000 variants for VQSR. I think we should also have a maximum number of VCFs per batch so we do not increase our memory requirements too much. Please let me know what you think about this plan!

Yes, IPv6 will be the issue. Unfortunately, IPv6 is not supported unless you use the TPROXY (Linux only) firewall method.

Going from memory here, before you use TPROXY you need to run the following commands as root. This only needs to be done once:

```
ip route add local default dev lo table 100
ip rule add fwmark 1 lookup 100
ip route show table 100
ip -6 route add local default dev lo table 100
ip -6 rule add fwmark 1 lookup 100
ip -6 route show table 100
```

You then need to run sshuttle (as root) and use the `--method=tproxy` option.

Unfortunately my tests this morning have failed, sshuttle isn't intercepting the outgoing connections. I haven't used the TPROXY method in some time, so not sure if I have forgotten something important or the latest kernel (4.2.0) I am using has changed something.

TPROXY is somewhat complicated to understand, and even worse to try and debug - will see if I can see what is going on.

Hi @vsanchez2187,

Normally when you do a checkout in an E-Commerce website you choose your payment provider, do some stuff you need to do in advance (register your payment, add success and error callbacks etc) and then redirect to the payment page of the specific provider.

In Oscar API this is no different. However, you need to implement this yourself because of the major difference in payment provider API's. The API is expecting you that you will register a view with the name `api-payment` so it will return the correct url with all callback functions etc. What should be done with this or how you implement this is up to you. 

See also the notes here: http://django-oscar-api.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage/communicate_with_the_api.html#place-an-order-checkout  (the note about `payment_url`).

It is a little clunky and makes the datasource larger, but I think this adding this style literal dictionary/object as a property for each feature is the most straightforward way to implement this in Python. It allows us to use Leaflet's `setStyle` method to call a function and loop through the set of features to set each feature's style directly using the `style` key in each feature's `properties`, which it maps to nicely without any additional parsing. You can see where this happens in the `GeoJson` template [here](https://github.com/python-visualization/folium/blob/master/folium/features.py#L382).

Keeping this part of the transformation on the Python side allows us to have more direct control over the data directly vs writing a JS function like `getColor` (line 26) and `style` (line 37) like in [this fiddle](https://jsfiddle.net/jaseface87/o67xc00h/26/) could be challenging to do in a Jinja2 template that can flexibly handle a variety of inputs. It seems like would have to be pretty verbose, since it would involve writing a template JS function to call other JS functions to evaluate for the properties - how `style` calls `getColor` in the fiddle.

I only see the current situation as problematic if you already have properties named `style` or `highlight` in your GeoJson feature properties that are being overwritten in the `self.data` update, or if the size of the data is absolutely critical.

I'm still learning JS and definitely not an expert, however. Do you have any ideas on how to implement this in folium?

Hi Jason, that sounds good! I think it's a nice idea to have different tooltips for each feature, instead of one text for the entire thing. And to also have the option to use popups per feature.

The [Leaflet documentation on GeoJson objects](https://leafletjs.com/reference-1.3.0.html#geojson) already contains an example that enables popups with a differing text per feature. It seems the same way also works for tooltips. That way you won't have to deal with the mouseover events. But in general, if you know how to do it in Leaflet, we can add it to folium.

For the Python part it seems smart to take the same approach as how `style_function` and `highlight_function` are implemented. That is, the needed values are added to the geojson data so they are available in the javascript functions. 

I'm not sure what would be the best way to also enable more complicated popups, such as vega. Of course you can put in raw html, but it would be nice if we can come up with a way to enable the use of folium `Popup` and `Vega` objects.

They differ in how they affect serialization. `only` is used to filter serialized output. `partial` has no effect on serialization; it only allows missing fields to pass validation (and deserialization), even if the field is required.

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class MySchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.Field(required=True)
    bar = fields.Field(required=True)


only_schema = MySchema(only=('foo', ))

print(only_schema.dump({'foo': 42, 'bar': 24}))

partial_schema = MySchema(partial=True)

print(partial_schema.dump({'foo': 42, 'bar': 24}))
```

Output:

```python
MarshalResult(data={'foo': 42}, errors={})
MarshalResult(data={'bar': 24, 'foo': 42}, errors={})
```

Then re-import the keys.  Note that if you have other apps using south as well, this will break those as well.

This request essentially asks to implement the PVsyst model for a-Si, which includes a new term to account for recombination loss in the intrinsic layer (see [http://files.pvsyst.com/help/thinfilm_recombinationloss.htm](url)

PVsyst provides a default value for the single new parameter (di^2/mu teff = 1.4V).

Implementing this new model depends on having `calcparams_PVsyst` see #470 and will require a new function to solve the diode equation with the recombination current term.

I would raise a word of caution about calculating the missing component from the two that are given among GHI, DNI and DHI.  In real-world data they rarely add up perfectly.  If you have only one of the three, ignorance is bliss and you can model the other two so they do add up perfectly.  But if you have two, then the third one may end up being negative or some other unreasonable value.  Also, dividing by cos(zenith) where zenith -> 90 is a sensitive operation.

RRTM is used widely in forecasting/climate models. It's a correlated-k model (see [this paper](http://irina.eas.gatech.edu/EAS8803_Fall2007/Fu_Liou1992.pdf) for example), which is calibrated using a line-by-line model that has been validated with observations. As far as I'm aware it is very accurate. I used the model for some fairly theoretical work with no clouds or aerosols, but the model does support these and is very comprehensive.

The python wrapper I wrote (which is pyrrtm as mentioned above) might be a useful starting point for someone who wanted to call the model from python. However, I did write it with my more theoretical use in mind so doesn't currently support clouds or aerosols. Adding these would probably be quite straightforward.

Just for some context:

The reason for the `ValueError` is how the inputs to `net` are retrieved. When you call `inp1, inp2 = net.input_layers` , you will actually get the DenseLayers as `inp1` and `inp2`, because `Layer.input_layer` is defined as the preceding layer, not the actual `InputLayer` for the network.

Because the DenseLayers are considered the input, when the line `out2 = L.get_output(net, {inp1:xx,inp2:yy})` is run, my understanding is that `xx` and `yy` are effectively treated as outputs from the DenseLayer into the ElemwiseSumLayer, hence the `ValueError`.

The quickest fix is to make `net_one` and `net_two` into dictionary objects, so that we can later retrieve their inputs directly. E.G. 
```
net_one = {}
net_one['in'] = L.InputLayer((1, None, 1), x)
net_one['rl'] = L.RecurrentLayer(net_one['in'], 7, only_return_final=True)
net_one['dl'] = L.DenseLayer(net_one['rl'], 1, nonlinearity=None, b=None, num_leading_axes=0)

net_two = {}
net_two['in'] = L.InputLayer((1, None, 1),y)
net_two['rl'] = L.RecurrentLayer(net_two['in'], 7, only_return_final=True)
net_two['dl'] = L.DenseLayer(net_two['rl'], 1, nonlinearity=None, b=None, num_leading_axes=0)

net = L.ElemwiseSumLayer((net_one['dl'], net_two['dl']))
print(L.get_output_shape(net))

# Input layers
inp1 = net_one['in']

> It requires manually updating the version string for each release

Which they sell as as a feature. They check whether the declared version in `setup.py` matches the version in `__version__` and the latest `v*.*` git tag and refuse uploading to pip if there is a mismatch.

> but it's a lot simpler and still provides revision information.

Even for git archives, it seems. Not sure if that would include [all the `pip install git:...` forms](http://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/2crput/how_to_install_with_pip_directly_from_github/), though, versioneer might have an edge here because it immortalizes the version as a fixed string on `setup.py install`. Also I don't really like that it makes `__version__` a `Version` class instance instead of a string, but that seems easy to change.

We're getting closer... I'd still very much like a `0.11+2.g1076c97` style version independent of how it's installed. The basic ingredients seem to be `git describe --long --match 'v*.* --dirty` (to get a version string from a git checkout), the `export-subst` git attribute (to materialize a version string in `git archive`), and some solution for `setup.py install` (to rescue a version string from a git checkout into an installed version).

Oh, and by the way, another reason for having a descriptive `__version__` was to be able to make #262 work correctly. This needs at least a distinction between "release x.y" and "latest master" (which is probably the minimum we'd all agree on supporting anyway, but which could be solved with a simple string in `__init__.py`).

To me, there are already very few people working on binaries. and only a small fraction of them really understand what binary analysis (and symbolic execution) is about. There will be users get attracted by the over-simplified decorator-based hooking grammar used in Manticore's examples, and those users can very well become fluent angr users in the near future, if they are attracted to angr instead of Manticore. Therefore I believe that at least it is necessary to take in this PR.

Specifically, I don't see it as "we are trying to simplify angr's interface to cater to users who want a simpler interface". I see it as "an exciting entry-level example to attract potential angr users". If users want to use more functionalities in hooking, they should switch to the properly designed, though more complex hooking interfaces.

Hi,
Is your server opensource? I'd be very much interested to try and debug. Current version supports v1.1 chunked framing and has been tested against netopeer and Cisco ASR devices.
So, handshake begins with a hello msg 
```
# send a HELLO msg using v1.0 EOM markers.
validated_element(data, tags='{urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0}hello')
data = "%s%s"%(data, MSG_DELIM)
```
(https://github.com/ncclient/ncclient/blob/master/ncclient/transport/ssh.py#L523) 
using the "]]>]]>" delimiter 
```
MSG_DELIM = "]]>]]>"
```
(https://github.com/ncclient/ncclient/blob/master/ncclient/transport/ssh.py#L37). 

If "urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.1" is found in server capabilities, then for the remainder of the session the chunk framing mechanism is used 
```
if self._server_capabilities:
                            if 'urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.1' in self._server_capabilities and 'urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.1' in self._client_capabilities:
                                logger.debug("Selecting netconf:base:1.1 for encoding")
self._parse11()
```
(https://github.com/ncclient/ncclient/blob/master/ncclient/transport/ssh.py#L510)

For the definition of _parse11, check here: https://github.com/ncclient/ncclient/blob/master/ncclient/transport/ssh.py#L136. 

Could you set loglevel to debug and paste the output? I'd like to make sure that the chunking mechanism on the client side works as expected and that 1.1 is indeed the parsing mode selected by the client.

Thanks in advance,

L.

OK, in summary the main changes in this branch are:

 * Using `__slots__` in a lot more classes.
This at the very least improves garbage collection but also means we have a feeling for what objects each class has at a class level more quickly.
 * Added the `_additional_slots` attribute.
This allows for inherited classes to add additional objects to the `__slots__` from the base class easily.
 * Added the `getNew` method to GangaObject.
This allows to get an object of an inherited type which has been fully instantiated correctly without having to fully define the attributes of a class from the schema. This is useful when creating an empty object to be populated with attributes from storage such as an XML file.
* Fixed  a serious performance regression in the construction of simple objects through the Proxy.
There was a loop where objects were being constructed and overloaded multiple times within the proxy construction layer which was slowing the construction of new class objects from within the string parser and IPython interface.
* Fixed a race condition in the credentials caching.
Creating a new cache was not thread safe and appeared to be at times sub-optimal. I've added a global thread lock around cache operations as well as some additional logic to ensure information read from disk is correctly cached and returned from memory where applicable.
* Added the ability for generic backends to parse multiple jobs in parallel using queues.
The code required to monitor/update a job has been demanded to be thread-safe through this interface for a while so I've allowed for parsing multiple jobs in parallel to speed up monitoring. This is good on backends where monitoring many jobs at once causes performance problems.
This expands on the existing logic to prevent overloading the backends with too many large serial queries and allows for multiple parallel short number of jobs to be monitored whilst not consuming all worker threads within the queues system.

These were all problems highlighted from trying to fix profiling tests where various pieces of code were performing poorly. In particular the issues I was able to address were:
* Monitoring would capture a job for monitoring but then be very slow to proceed to monitor further jobs or taking a long time to monitor many subjobs. (fixed by multi-threaded monitoring)
* Reading in a large job from disk took >60sec (This is now better but is still a few sec). (fixed by `getNew`)
* Creating many new objects from in front of the GPI took orders of magnitude longer than from behind the proxy. (fixed by bug in proxy layer)

As well as this I've fixed some annoying papercuts:
* Making better errors rather than throwing from asserts hidden deep in the codebase to the user
* Fixing annoying bug with credentials system where if you pass the wrong object you get a stack trace
* Fixing bug where credentials weren't satisfied ahead of a parallel submit from the baseclass. (This meant the submit fails due to not being able to construct a credential from a queue).

I appreciate it's a rather large single commit so if people have the time to review the changes I'd like to push for 6.7 with this.

    def __init__(self):

```
This data will exist as long as that Python object exists in memory.

Hi @deusxmachina42 
Due to how .NET interoperability works you need both Intel.RealSense.dll and realsense2.dll at run-time. 
However, only the Intel.RealSense.dll is a .NET assembly you can (and should) add reference to. 
realsense2.dll is native (C++) library that must be found at runtime (at current directory usually). You can't directly reference it in your C# project. 
Please note that you also must select architecture (x86 or x64) that matches how realsense2.dll was compiled. Using Any CPU is not recommended when embedding native components.

The projected IR pattern cannot be controlled or modified electronically, apart from simply adjusting the power. However, using external projectors is fine as long as it
1. Is on either DC (continuously) or at >50Khz. 
2. If it uses lasers all care should be taken to reduce laser speckle. One option is to move the projector for example. This can actually be beneficial and help reduce the depth ripple a bit if you use averaging as well.
3. It is eye safe (of course)
4. Wavelength is between 400 and 1000nm
5. Pattern should not be periodic, but semi-random. A pattern of between 5000 and 300000 dots will work. 
6. No extra calibration is needed by the RealSense camera. It does not care about the nature of the projector. 
7. Multiple projectors can be used.  This will help with occlusions.  There are no real constraints otherwise on where you place the projectors. 
8. The projectors do not have to be on same stiffener as the RealSense camera.

This quickest way to be sure your kernel is patched is to run this with the camera plugged in.

    v4l2-ctl --list-formats --device=/dev/videoX

Where X is the device node for the depth camera. With the F200 you get only one named format if it isn't patched, and a bunch if it is.

I'm pretty sure you got the right format though. The way to get depth, and infrared at the same time on the F200 is with a muxed 24 bit format. The first 16 bits are depth, and the last 8 are infrared. In both the depth, and IR images there is banding which tells me you got the depth data. There are also shadows that aren't from the projector getting blocked which tells me you got the IR data. 

I think the unpacking routine is moving 32 bits at a time instead of 24. It's taking me a while to grasp how it's supposed to work with the templates. You could try using your own unpacking routine. One byte array in, and two out. Once you have the array of two byte values filled cast it to 16 bit.

Thoughts from today's meeting:

I think a good approach is for us to continue using the same Pulp Smash Python package. This will make the workflow for our users, Jenkins jobs, etc easier. It will also allow us to re-use some tooling. For example, pulp_smash.{config,selectors,api,cli} can all largely be re-used.

Most or all of our tests for Pulp 2 will no longer work with Pulp 3. Given this, I think a good approach is for us to create new (namespace) packages within Pulp Smash, like `pulp_smash.tests.*.api_v3`, and to place new tests there. That way, entire packages of code can be run or skipped, which is fairly easy to understand and implement.

Tangent: I'd like to pull constants related to Pulp Fixtures out of Pulp Smash and into a separate (installable) package - preferably, right in the source code with Pulp Fixtures. This would let Pulp Smash be more focused, and it would let others re-use Pulp Fixtures if they so desired.

        # Make the window's context current
        glfw.make_context_current(self._window)
        if glfw.window_should_close(self._window):
            glfw.terminate()

        self.image = self.sim.render(640,480).astype(np.float) / 255.0

        # Render here, e.g. using pyOpenGL
        self.texture = glGenTextures(1)
        glEnable(GL_TEXTURE_2D)
        glPixelStorei(GL_UNPACK_ALIGNMENT, 1)
        glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, self.texture)
        glTexParameterf(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MIN_FILTER, GL_LINEAR)
        glTexParameterf(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_MAG_FILTER, GL_LINEAR)
        glTexParameterf(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_S, GL_CLAMP)
        glTexParameterf(GL_TEXTURE_2D, GL_TEXTURE_WRAP_T, GL_CLAMP)
        glTexEnvf(GL_TEXTURE_ENV, GL_TEXTURE_ENV_MODE, GL_MODULATE)
        glTexImage2D(GL_TEXTURE_2D, 0,
                     GL_RGB,
                     self.image.shape[1], self.image.shape[0], 0,
                     GL_RGB,
                     GL_FLOAT,
                     self.image)
        glDisable(GL_TEXTURE_2D)

        glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT)
        glClearColor(0., 0., 0., 0.)
        glClearDepth(1)
        glMatrixMode(GL_MODELVIEW)
        glLoadIdentity()
        glBindTexture(GL_TEXTURE_2D, self.texture)
        glEnable(GL_TEXTURE_2D)
        # draw a textured quad, shrink it a bit so the edge is clear
        glBegin(GL_QUADS)
        glTexCoord2f(0., 0.)
        glVertex3f(-0.9, -0.9, 0.)
        glTexCoord2f(1., 0.)
        glVertex3f(0.9, -0.9, 0.)
        glTexCoord2f(1., 1.)
        glVertex3f(0.9, 0.9, 0.)
        glTexCoord2f(0., 1.)
        glVertex3f(-0.9, 0.9, 0.)
        glEnd()
        glDisable(GL_TEXTURE_2D)

        # Swap front and back buffers
        glfw.swap_buffers(self._window)

        # Poll for and process events
        glfw.poll_events()

Implementing crystals is a non-trivial task since tensorflow does not natively support changing the graph structure mid training, but it can be done by running one sessions with an all-pairs model (constructed using the layer API), then extracting the torsion values out of the snapshot of the trained all-pairs model, and then using that to construct the final ensemble.

I suggest instead using the embedded tiny lattices already provided in this repo. These models do not do explicit feature selection/arrangement, but rather use a linear embedding of all input features for each lattice in the ensemble. Note that this is a strict superset of the feature selection (since a linear embedding can represent a subset selection), so you might in fact get better results out of ETLs compared to crystals (at some cost to model complexity and speed).

> Retrieving rows from Tensors is O(log *n*) and BigTensors is O(*n* log *n*).

Here, *n* is the number of rows that you’re retrieving, or the number of rows in the table? From "just do a O(log *n*) search, read 20kb off disk, and we’re done" it sounds like the latter, but surely if that’s the case than nothing should take ω(*n*) time, right? (You can always just read the whole table sequentially, which is Θ(*n*)!)

If I `SELECT tensor FROM Tensors WHERE rowid = ?` vs. `SELECT tensor FROM BigTensors WHERE rowid = ?`, where `rowid` is `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` for both, what causes the difference?

The Tensors table is designed to offer contiguous data storage. For example, the scalars dashboard might display dozens of plots, and each plot requires a thousand or more rows be retrieved. Since each row for a scalar about 20 bytes, we just do a O(logn) search, read a 20kb off disk, and we're done.

> I use scanning to describe queries like `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Tensors` that aren't indexed and have to brute their way through lots of table data to get an answer. The smaller rows are, the quicker something like that can be computed. That's probably why MySQL's authors designed things the way they did.

In the spirit of reducing RAM requirements (possibly at the cost of code requirements), I was actually thinking about passing the port value as a part of `CoapServer::Start()`.  No reason to keep that value around in memory.

For the `CoapClient` behavior, I think what you have here is fine.

> For number 1 above, since the signature of the API is still changing, all the consumers of the API in openthread will have to be updated. What do you want to do with them? Add the code to call into `otInstanceInit` and pass the resulting otInstance in, or do you just want to pass in NULL for that change? 

The former: The consumers would be updated to act like they are doing something when under the hood they aren't actually doing anything.

> For number 2, I am a bit unclear what is happening. Since some of the functions don't need the `otInstance`, wouldn't they just not be updated in number 1?

They wouldn't need the `otInstance` specifically, but they are still operating on some sort of object that is conceptually independent from `otInstance`. This commit is where those objects are fleshed out.

The library does not expect the user to convert their data into a string and pass that string into publishEvent().

An XML codec would need to use a library like https://pypi.python.org/pypi/dicttoxml to convert between a dictionary object and a XML string.

> In a given install environment, only one version of python can exist at a time. The python version found by cmake at install time.

Yes.  That's exactly the problem.

With this patch, the install environment python version is the only one that can be used by cmake pybind11-using projects, while the python version to build against is supposed to be configurable (e.g. by setting `PYTHON_EXECUTABLE` in a project's `CMakeLists.txt` before the `find_package(pybind11)`).  As long as `pybind11Config.cmake` is being installed in a location that isn't specific to the python version, it's going to be impossible to have two projects that use `find_package(pybind11)` with one depending on Python 2 and the other on Python 3 on the same system: If my pybind11 install environment used Python 2, my Python 3 project would end up with the Python 2 include path; if it was Python 3, my Python 2 project ends up with the Python 3 include path.

Given that there can only be one `pybind11Config.cmake`, some ways that could make this work are:
1. move most of the pybind11Config logic into a cmake script that gets installed in a python-version-specific location, then make the main pybind11Config.cmake just detect the python version and dispatch to the version-specific cmake script;
2. install the headers somewhere where the include path will be specific to pybind11;
3. don't install a global pybind11Config.cmake at all; or
4. achieve the desired symmetry by always installing to ${PREFIX}/include (which seems pretty reasonable given that there is absolutely nothing python-version-dependent in the header files).
